# Thread: your/our true essence....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-our-true-essence.5264/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2012-12-26T16:22:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-our-true-essence.5264/post-68443>

Do you think our true Â essence is apparent when we write? Â Does it happen once we become more comfortable - Â or Â - Â can we conceal who we really are Â ..... Â or..... is it always blatantly obvious?

I am struck by the fact .....well I think it is a fact.... that when you spend time with strangers Â - they see you for who you are ...your real essence. Â Not all strangers of course! Â I seem to gravitate toward lovely people who are kind, generous and have a huge heart. Â I feel so enriched.

I have travelled a bit the last few years Â - Â my Doctor says it is because I am running away. Â At first I listened to her but now I have decided that that is not correct at all. Â I am finding that I can be my true self. Â  Â It feels like the real and true me and I have made amazing friends ....I think because I am being myself .... but I am myself at home .... so I guess I don't really understand this at all.

I think you can see into someone's heart pretty quickly ...... maybe that is way to simplistic.....

Whaddayouthink????

### janagram — 2012-12-27T13:14:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-our-true-essence.5264/post-68496>

well, strangers don't carry all the memories, hurts, opinions, etc., which would make them possibly more "in the moment". Thereby seeing you "in the moment" minus the baggage of history.

### diane — 2012-12-27T16:16:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-our-true-essence.5264/post-68520>

I know people who can tell right away if they are drawn to someone or whether they are not a fan. Maybe this is more judging behaviour, but I do think that intuition comes into play. I've alway relied on my intuition over the years. Of course with people, I tend to give everyone the benefit of the doubt. Until I get burned LOL. It's actually one of the things I like about myself, so I'll take being wrong in with the many times I'm right!Â  { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

There are, of course, some people I just down gel with right off the bat. And it may not be that they don't have a good essence, and they may be a good person, but there's personality or something that doesn't sit right.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-12-28T00:37:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-our-true-essence.5264/post-68555>

Last week I went out to buy some new jeans and I was wearing a bright yellow shirt with a really bold necklace. Â It was overcast and a little misty outside. Â A lady came up to me and told me I was wearing her color. Â She told me her name was Sunshine and we chatted a bit, asked each other a few questions. Â Somewhere in there she told me that her middle name was Dawn, Â because that's when the sunshine comes back out of the darkness. Â Turns out that 20 years ago she spent a year in a coma and it changed her life. Â She had a lot of jokes and was very happy, Â but I could tell that, well... Â she wasn't "all there". Â Her brother had brought her shopping and you could just tell that he loved her and was happy to let her indulge in chatting away with strangers even though I could tell that he was a little reserved about what my reaction might be. Â 

She had grabbed my hand and held on tight the whole time we talked.... Â  and then I gave her a hug before heading on my way. Â 

I guess we had something to share that day, Â I definitely felt like she gave me a piece of light. Â I'm sure her given first name wasn't Sunshine, and her middle name wasn't Dawn, but I think maybe it was her \*real\* name. Â Maybe our only true name is the one we give ourselves? Â  Or is Sunshine just her true essence? Â Â 

I don't know... Â  but it was a very human experience.

### Charlotte — 2012-12-28T09:20:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-our-true-essence.5264/post-68578>

This is a really intriguing question, Caroline and I am wondering a lot myself.

I am, what you might call a straight forward person, too fast often to say what I think (which got me into a lot of trouble). However, what I noticed about myself is, that very often I wear the so called "pink glasses" seeing people only as how I want to see them, always depending on in which kind of place I am at the moment. So I have become quite careful about my own interpretations of somebody else. Sometimes I meet people and we are hitting if off immediately and sometimes not.

But I have learned not to trust my own feelings about someone else. I have a friend whom I know since many years and who has a son exactly the same age as my oldest son. I changed his diapers already when he was a baby. He had a hard life in some ways with his Mom being alcoholic (but nonetheless doing the best she could for her children) and his Dad not caring. So his expectancy of choices for his own life was limited. As he could not find a job in his profession, he learned another, not being able to care for his daughter (now 13), I supported him financially during his apprenticeship and gave him a job, after he was done. He lived at my house for 3 years with his daughter and I paid the bills. He was in a way like a son. When I got into trouble with my company, he was the only one that immediately quit and got another job. I was dumbfounded at first and then had to think very hard, what i had missed.

What I found out was, that I expected the same from him, that I gave. Loyalty, gratitude and to stick by me. Well, he did not, and I realized that I was not looking closely especially not at my own expectations. After I got beyong the disappointment I realized that he could not act any other way, as these were just not traits he ever learned or considered rewarding.

What do I think now? He is made of the same essence we are all made of, but it is just buried beneath a lot of conditioning, fear and losses. Sometimes, the light shines through and sometimes it just does not. Same goes to all of us. So when we meet someone else and it clicks we might be in a similar space at that time. The more we peel away our own conditioning, fear and losses the brighter the light can shine for all to see. So I concentrate on me, getting optimal, so I do not need glasses so to speak through which I look at the world, becoming clear minded, clear hearted and then, maybe, I can see clearly also.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-28T10:45:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-our-true-essence.5264/post-68581>

That is a starfish perspective Charlotte....excellent.

### Charlotte — 2012-12-28T15:25:33+0200

you are, Inger, believe me you are. And you are doing so very well, indeed.Â  Life has a way to open our eyes. Once open, there is no way to close them again. ### caroline — 2012-12-31T01:48:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-our-true-essence.5264/post-68795>

I love reading these insightful comments ....I guess this was more personal for me than I had originally thought.

About 2 years ago I spent 12 hours hours on a train going to Bejiing - with a lovely young man. Â We shared so much about each other and had some great laughs. Â When I got home I got the most beautiful email from him Â ...... he had gotten my true essence.

It was so empowering. Â In my day to day life I have some negative people and it can cause me to doubt myself. Â But this gorgeous young man gave me a remarkable gift and I will be forever grateful for his kind and generous words. He kind of gave me power...... I know who I am ...... Â mostly...... but it was a validation. Â Yes that is me and I can't let anyone take it away.

He also gave me something else Â ..... I now talk to everyone I see ... standing in line at supermarkets, on airplanes, in waiting areas ... there is an endless number of very interesting people and I am not afraid to speak in case they won't like me or whatever. Â Now - you do have to respect people's boundaries of course Â .. but generally ... people are such a joy ...and I have so much fun. The craziest things always are happening - pure magic!Â

### Inger — 2012-12-31T03:56:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-our-true-essence.5264/post-68800>

Caroline! It damned on me... when I read your post above.. what if the people we meet that feels like a gift/an angel as you describe the young man above - what if they posess so much love, and they see the beauty in you.. and as they do that, it is like magic and it expands..? In you?

What if that is a true gift of healing, to see the beauty in the other, and then it becomes more, and more?

But how can we learn this beautiful skill? Because often we see the things that are wrong

and ugly. I do not know how I could explain what I am sensing but I vaguely sense this is something very beautiful. I wonder if that is what happends when we are healthy in our brain and yes.. then also in our minds, body and spirit.

When I recall the most beautiful moments in my life.. it was when beeing together whith someone that saw the beauty in me hat I could not even see... but through this persons eyes I become a better person.. it was like getting lifted up. Thinking about these moments gave me courage when I had none. To do the good things, that demanded a lot from me. It was like beeing loved gave me all the powers I needed. It still does.

I need to think about this now. How I can give other in my surroundings this gift.. I want to become so much more able to it!

I want it in the deepest of my heart, because I know nothing as beautiful as that. It feels like... love. Caroline, I know you understand!

And yeah.. I sometimes get so angry at myself how I could have let myself be so broken.. I have messed up so many things in my life. OMG it HURTS to think about it. Just the fact I have met not only one absolutely amazing man that was so intrested in knowing me better, but I after a while turned him down to stay by hubby that was only destroying me. Because I was scared to leave him. Now, I am alone and right now have quite zero possibilities to get to know any men at all. It is kind of so sad because I love men.Â I love sex. I loved to be a wife. But. I need to think forwards now. NOT looking back! Not on the things I did wrong and that I regret, but instead think about all the love I recieve/recieved and let it carry me on. On the healing path. To concentrate on my own healing and loving myself. Only when I have healed I am able to help others, and it does feel liberating to give myself that permission. It feels like peace deep inside. Because things have to happend in the right order to be fruitful.

I tell myself, I have to walk the steps.

There are no "shortcut road" to heaven.

Charlotte.. I think that is when we find gold... embracing the pain and all as you said above. Because that is when we take no shortcuts to have it easier.

### caroline — 2013-01-01T17:54:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-our-true-essence.5264/post-68942>

Inger and Charlotte you said so many profound things - I keep turning them over and over in my mind. I think it is magic and love that one person can give to another. It doesn't cost us anything and it expands our heart and makes us a better being. It is also unexpected and it makes people glow. The other day I helped an elderly lady choose a new hat in a department store and we had so much fun ... both of us were giggling - for me ... it doesn't get much better than that. I really was the beneficiary of far more than I could possibly describe.

i have a silly supermarket story ... I was checking out at the supermarket and I asked the cashier to open my bottle of wine for me. I had never had her before and she was incredulous and said in a very loud voice - 3 times ....YOU WANT ME TO OPEN THIS BOTTLE OF WINE???? NOW????? Because she was so loud - everyone in 2 checkout lines heard her and became involved! . I proceeded to explain to her and everyone that I am on my own and I can never open the dam bottles!!! It is okay to have a jar of pickles that you can't open ... but it is NOT OKAY to not be able to open a bottle of wine - for the love of god! All these people became in such a good mood ... they thought we were going to have a wine tasting etc. I got many offers of phone numbers if I ever needed help .LOL. Such a silly thing caused so much joy and inter action among all these strangers. A few years ago I would have been mortified and humiliated and extremely embarrassed and slunk out of the store ......instead I was grinning from ear to ear!

### janagram — 2013-01-02T16:29:23+0200

and, I love how Jack reflects their light.

### caroline — 2013-01-02T16:54:40+0200

### Charlotte — 2013-01-02T17:27:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-our-true-essence.5264/post-69055>

What I nice story, Caroline. I would love to meet you in a supermarket, we would have a blast. I am also always chatting with the ladies at the counter and it makes me feel good to make them laugh and laugh with them. I am not a friend of having friendship cards, collection love hearts to glue into a little book and get a gift when it is full that you don't need, membership cards of any sort, as every supermarket, pharmacy, shop now has them. And everytime I go shopping, I get asked if I have one and if I want one. I especially like the ones that give out cards for being a faithful customer. So everytime my answer to their question is: no, I am not faithful. First I get a strange look, then I explain and we start laughing. How could anybody be faithful with so many cards in their wallet?
Inger, I am thinking a lot about you. Guess what, if you attracted nice people already when you considered yourself very damaged, just think who you will attract when you are not? And it gets even better than that because when you are optimal, people will enter into your life that you considered beyond your reach just for you to pick out the best for you. And then, the question is not anymore if they are interested in you, but if YOU are interested in them. And no one and nothing will keep you from reaching out and taking it. You are now already reaching fully around the globe attracting people from at least 3 continents. And you did not have to move one inch from your beautiful little place. So how is that for a success story?

### Charlotte — 2013-01-03T09:56:11+0200

hey Inger, you already know what is inside the onion, don't you? The americans have a saying which goes: you cannot have your cake and eat it too. And I say, you can have your cake and eat it too. Isn't that wonderful?
This essence of you is not broken or damaged. It is vibrant, healthy and beautiful and always will be and has been. What I think is that we must not identify too much with the 'peels', and they are not YOU. YOU that is beyond illness, brokenness, damage and that goes for all of us. So I like to concentrate on the health, not the disease, to bring my inner light to a burning furnace, just burning off the peels that keep me apart from the rest of the world.

### [email redacted] — 2012-12-27T22:08:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/iron-overload-hemochromatosis.5262/post-68550>

High iron and hemochromatosis have been on my project list for a while now, but I have had some success in reducing free iron.

A DNA test has shown that I am a carrier with a single hereditary hemochromatosis gene. In March of this year (2012), my hematologist wouldn't quite declare hemochromatosis because one of my numbers didn't quite pan out and was in the normal range. They were as follows:

TIBC - 237 Low (250-450)

UIBC -Â  47 Low (150-375)

Iron, Serum - 190 High (40-155)

Iron Saturation - 80% Alert (15-55%)

What screws up a perfect hemochromatosis diagnosis is that ferritin was in the normal range:

Ferritin, Serum - 287 (30-400)

Exactly three months later follow up tests showed everything in the normal range except slightly low TIBC:

TIBC - 245 Low (250-450)Â 

UIBC - 159 (150-375)

Iron, Serum - 86 (40-155)

Iron Saturation - 35% (15-55)

Ferritin, Serum - 220 (30-400)

The first set of iron tests were done because I snuck the tests onto a lab order for some different tests by the endocrinologist I was seeing for different reasons (thyroid - multinodular, Hashimoto's), but when he saw the results he sent me to a hematologist. It was during this time waiting to see the hematologist that I researched high iron and two weeks after I got the first results I went and gave blood at the Red Cross but they won't let you come back for two months, so during that time I began supplementing with IP6 (inositol hexaphosphate or rice bran extract) which chelates free iron out of the blood and leaves bound iron alone.

[http://www.lewrockwell.com/sardi/sardi39.html](http://http://www.lewrockwell.com/sardi/sardi39.html)

All this was done on the sly as I didn't tell any doctor what I was doing because I was between doctors. But by the time of the 2nd set of tests the hematologist ordered were back the high free iron was in the normal range. So was it the IP6 that did it?Â The hematologist, before I mentioned that I took the IP6, said that the Hashimoto's might have been interfering somehow, but it sounded to me like he was grasping at straws.

So I don't really know except that I can say this - I also have idiopathic peripheral neuropathy and was getting some pretty strong cramping in my lower calves and ankles and feet. High iron can eat up oxygen in circulation and I noticed that gradually, as I was taking the IP6, the cramping began to let up. I still had some low level more infrequent cramping but taking selenium seems to have abated that altogether now. High iron also causes weakness and fatigue and I also began to notice these going away. The weakness is gone (I thought my gym days were almost over), but I still have a few fatigue issues, what with the thyroid and also recent tests show low testosterone, so these are the current projects.

A neurologist had me on Lyrica for over a year for the neuropathy, but I quit gluten and in less then a year I was weaned off the Lyrica because theÂ flame was turned down to a pilot light.

So if you haven't tried the IP6, I'm just throwingÂ this out there that it might be a worthÂ a look.

Ed

### Optimalbound — 2012-12-27T12:31:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-what-does-everyone-make-of-jimmy-moores-latest-podcast-with-the-grand-wizard.5266/post-68490>

I'll always be grateful to Moore for his podcasts with Dr.K, as they played an important role in making me study the jackkruse.com blog and eventually follow the protocols, but this latest interview with the Grand Wizard does seem a bit ill-adviced. I don't know if Jimmy actually shares Duke's views or if he is just happy to spread the lowcarb message to anyone who will listen, and thinks that everyone deserves to be healthy, including rabid anti-semites. Â If the latter is the case, I think Jimmy perhaps underestimates the power of guilt-by-association and how it might detract from his message and his credibility. Â  (Dsicalimer: I live on the other side of the Atlantic, so there are quite possibly any number of cultural and political aspects to this story that I am totally unaware of.)

BTW, I noticed that Jimmy recently tweeted about Inger's high meat video:

https://twitter.com/livinlowcarbman/status/282867484426641408Â

### DarleenMB — 2012-12-27T12:47:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-what-does-everyone-make-of-jimmy-moores-latest-podcast-with-the-grand-wizard.5266/post-68492>

I think there's a misunderstanding here ... Moore did not interview Duke ... he was invited onto Duke's radio show to talk about low-carb. Â There is a difference.

### janagram — 2012-12-27T12:52:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-what-does-everyone-make-of-jimmy-moores-latest-podcast-with-the-grand-wizard.5266/post-68493>

Optimalbound, it's true, you may not fully understand our home grown Grand Wizardry, and though it sounds comical, it's not...it's fear and ignorance all rolled together...Oh, with guns!!

Bad call, Jimmy.Â

### kathylu — 2012-12-27T12:57:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-what-does-everyone-make-of-jimmy-moores-latest-podcast-with-the-grand-wizard.5266/post-68494>

I think it was a poor choice on Jimmy's part, though he certainly has a point when he says that DD's audience deserves to know the truth about nutrition too. Unfortunately, it leaves the unpleasant impression that he (JM) may agree with some of DD's views, whether or not he actually does.

### Charlotte — 2012-12-27T13:56:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-what-does-everyone-make-of-jimmy-moores-latest-podcast-with-the-grand-wizard.5266/post-68499>

I have never heard before of this Mr. Duke (because I am not American, I presume), so I am not hung up on the persons doing this interview.

Halfway through the interview I got bored and fastforwarded in order not to miss anything, but there were no real new revelations.

What I was sorely missing was some sound information. Even if I might not understand all of which is being said (not on this interview though) I would still like some more in-depth information. To me is was far too superficial. And what I ask myself is, that if Jimmy Moore is on this diet since 9 years and has lost a tremendous amount of weight in the beginning, why is he still weighing 245 pounds? The emphasis for me was too much on the food, the weight loss and not enough on the other beneficial results, except for diabetes, cholesterol and heart and no mention on the importance of fish and fresh and raw.

But what I also understood is, that JM is not an expert in the true sence of the word. Mr. Duke would have done much better to invite Dr. Kruse or some other specialist in the field as the information he would have gotten would have been much more thorough and deep.

However, the more people learn about low-carb diet the better and even better if they head the advice. So I guess the interview was good for that.

### DarleenMB — 2012-12-27T14:49:50+0200

> **Charlotte said**
> I have never heard before of this Mr. Duke (because I am not American, I presume), so I am not hung up on the persons doing this interview.

Charlotte, David Duke is a racist of the worst stripe and is the so-called Grand Wizard of the Ku Klux Klan. Â His views are loathsome, to put it mildly. Â THIS is why there is such a dust-up going on. Â If you don't know what the Klan is you can google it and get more info than you would ever want. Â They are vile.

Of course, that's just MY opinion.

### diane — 2012-12-27T16:59:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-what-does-everyone-make-of-jimmy-moores-latest-podcast-with-the-grand-wizard.5266/post-68530>

For me, it's important to live by my values, so I choose to associate with those that are consistent. My own values do not include racism.

However, everyone has to make their own choices. Not everyone thinks that way. And not everyone is conscious about their brand and what associations say about them. I'm guessing that now other brands are making this choice about Jimmy Moore.

I really don't know anything about Jimmy Moore, and it's not up to me to judge whether what he did was right or wrong. Only he can.

### Charlotte — 2012-12-28T12:26:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-what-does-everyone-make-of-jimmy-moores-latest-podcast-with-the-grand-wizard.5266/post-68589>

ahhh, thanks Darlene for the info. I know what the Ku-klux-klan is.

What also stuck to me from the interview was, that he seemed quite political and he liked to listen to himself talk. So even interviewing someone else, it was all about about him.

Nevertheless if some of his followers who listen to his show heed the advice and start eating paleo or even epi-paleo, it might even change their views.

I come from a country with a dark history of just that, it weren't the african americans, but the jews. Now, 80 years later it still lingers and we are still a guilt ridden people of which our politicans keepÂ  remembering us and to where my generation and the following generations now are sick of hearing it and feeling guilty and paying for something we did not even do. That is what guilt does. Now we have a new Nazi movement in Germany exactly because of that. People feel guilty and do not even exactly know what of and so they target it at the next best aim. In Italy they call it family vendetta. You kill my son, I kill yours and if you don't have one then your grandson. All it does is create more hatred. When I lived in America one day a man came up to me and called me a Nazi Bitch (somehow he realized I was German). I was shocked at so much hatred and violence with which he said that. So if we are not able to put our past (long or short) behind us, we stoke the same fire. The simple fact of 'being against something' is causing defensiveness and friction, friction causing fight and fight causing war. Being truly peaceful means to me, to embrace it all, the good and the bad, the beautiful and the ugly by realizing that all of it is always just both sides of the same coin. And what is the coin? Our judging mind.

So I do not know where this guy is coming from in his mind, but it cannot be a happy place and this and the conditioning he experienced made him to who he is right now. As a matter of fact, we all have been in his shoes at one time or another. It may not have been the same issues, but does that matter?

The good news is, that we all have the potential to change.

# Thread: Just made a super easy, super delicious, kick ass homemade clam chowder/cream of mushroom soup.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-made-a-super-easy-super-delicious-kick-ass-homemade-clam-chowder-cream-of-mushroom-soup.5267/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### furface — 2012-12-26T22:21:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-made-a-super-easy-super-delicious-kick-ass-homemade-clam-chowder-cream-of-mushroom-soup.5267/post-68463>

Wanting to add more shellfish to my Epi-Paleo diet, and so far not really falling in love with oysters, I decided to try and make a homemade clam chowder. On a whim, I decided to add some sliced white mushrooms and make it a combo clam chowder/cream of mushroom soup.

So I diced up about 6 slices of bacon, and fried it until crispy, but not burnt, removed the bacon, and transferred the grease into a medium sized pot/dutch oven.

I added about 1 tbsp of Kerrygold butter, then 2 stalks of celery diced, and 2 slices of diced onion. Let them saute for a bit, then added about a dozen thinly sliced mushrooms. Added some salt and pepper to taste.

After the mushrooms softened a bit, I drained two 14 oz cans of baby clams (kept the liquid), rinsed off the clams, set them aside.

I added the clam juice to the pot, let it heat for a bit, then added about 1 cup of whipping cream. Brought the mixture to a slight boil, reduced the heat, continued to stir and let it gently simmer/bubble for a few minutes.

Added the clams, more pepper, and continued to let it simmer/bubble for a few minutes until the clams were tender, but still a bit chewy. (That's the way I like them.)

Let it cook for about 5 minutes, removed from heat, poured a portion into a serving bowl.

Sprinkled some of the bacon on top, and enjoyed.

OMG so quick, so easy, and SOOOOO DELICIOUS!!

Just thought I'd share!

### furface — 2012-12-27T15:43:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-made-a-super-easy-super-delicious-kick-ass-homemade-clam-chowder-cream-of-mushroom-soup.5267/post-68516>

Thanks everyone. Sorry for being a bit impatient. lol

I actually consider whipping cream "fat" instead of dairy since there is very little if any carbohydrates in it. (That's my story and I'm sticking to it.)

I didn't have enough money for fresh clams as they cost about $1.00 each, and I wanted a lot of clams in my chowder. haha. Not to mention I don't have a clam shucker knife yet, plus I didn't want to wait hours by the time I'd open them, steam/cook them, etc etc But I will try it with fresh clams one of these days.

Not the biggest fan of heated dairy myself, but once in a while, for something like this, I don't mind.

Next time I might just saute some onions, mushrooms, bacon, and clams in a bit of grass fed butter and just eat it like that. Perhaps even toss in a few prawns as well.

I decided to make it all in a rush the other morning, to take to work for lunch. Unfortunately, already into it I discovered that I had no clams! I did have a tetra Pak of seafood stock and some frozen 'seafood medly' in the freezer, and that worked. had to make do with half and half, as well, so pretty thin, but still tasty! And quick! ### furface — 2013-01-05T14:39:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-made-a-super-easy-super-delicious-kick-ass-homemade-clam-chowder-cream-of-mushroom-soup.5267/post-69454>

No problem, Destiny, and Endless.

Glad your creations turned out well. That's the great thing about soups, stews, or chowders...it's a great way to clean out your fridge, or even freezer, by just throwing everything into the pot, and let it simmer, or "stew" for a few hours, or however long you cook it. Heck you could throw it all into a slow cooker, and let it slowly cook all day, and come home to a nice, healthy, tasty meal. Add whatever spices you want, and your done, and the stove, or slow cooker does all the work. haha

As far as eating or not eating dairy is concerned, I go by what I read in the Leptin Rx "approved" foods, and see that heavy whipping cream is listed, or considered a "fat" moreso than a "dairy" (but still have it in moderation, and not very often), so I don't really worry too much about it. At least that's my story, and I'm sticking to it. haha.

Like I've said before, I'm not going to over worry about every single little nit picky detail about everything. If I do 85-90% "properly", esp concerning my diet, and CT etc, a few "slip ups" or borderline food items every now and then, aren't really going to cause me to worry excessively. I think the stress of worrying would do far more damage to me than any possible damage the questionable item MIGHT end up doing, if it does any damage at all. But hey that's just me.

I'm getting ready to roast my first ever duck today!

Not only is it a great way to make a tasty meal, you almost always have leftovers left, which make for a super quick meal the next day or so, as all you have to do is reheat it. Not that I'm afraid to cook, or prepare something, but at times you don't feel like "slaving over a hot stove"

so you just have to reheat the leftover soup/stew/chowder, and voila, a quick, tasty, healthy, meal.

### endless — 2013-01-06T21:46:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-made-a-super-easy-super-delicious-kick-ass-homemade-clam-chowder-cream-of-mushroom-soup.5267/post-69681>

I decided to try this again dairy free. This time I sauteed an onion in bacon fat (would've used celery too but was out). Meanwhile I cooked a head of cauliflower in a litre of seafood stock till it was very soft, then blended it with my stick blender. I added a can of coconut milk, the onions, about 2 cups of sliced mushrooms, and a bag of frozen 'seafood medley' (clams, mussels, shrimp, squid, octopus). I added a teaspoon of red chili paste, a tsp of sea salt, tsp pepper, and Tbsp of fish sauce.

I know it's not ideal to use coconut with seafood, but it's something I will use occasionally. It's nice and creamy from the pureed cauliflower.

Oh, and of course I sprinkled bacon on it

CT is excellent for adrenal fatigue and thyroid problems. CT releases "stuff" out of the body like estrogen. But I think it can also free biotoxins and if your body can't handle this it could be cause for caution.Â  There may have been other stuff as well but most diseases CT helps.

### vkiernan — 2012-12-27T19:40:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-not-to-ct-adrenal-fatigue.5268/post-68536>

CT is not good when one is nursing.Â  Sends the toxins out into the breast milk and the baby gets them.

### MamaGrok — 2012-12-28T14:57:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-not-to-ct-adrenal-fatigue.5268/post-68604>

There are safe ways to detox while pregnant & breastfeeding. Â You just do it slowly. Â Instead of GAPS intro full blast for a month, you slowly remove one thing at a time until you're doing regular GAPS. Â Instead of working rapidly to an hour in 40F water every day, you work slowly, and maybe end up after a month or two in 55F water for just 20 minutes a couple of times Â a week. Â 

You watch for signs of excessive detox, like baby getting pimples, cranky, or sick. Â You back off if that happens; you take hot epsom salt baths with the baby; you start again more slowly. Â 

It's not impossible.

It can't be, because I (and the typical woman prior to 200 years ago) have spent most of my adult life either pregnant or nursing. Â It's the normal state for women between age 15 & 45, even though most of us don't do it anymore. Â I have to detox some time, so I do it very slowly, and I'm not the only one!

### Marie Negrila — 2014-03-25T03:16:24+0200

Interesting video.

Ok, so CT for low cortisol, but only when combined with Epi-Paleo, adherence to natural light cycles, avoidance of non-native EMF, & consumption of good (spirng/RO) water...

### omegajosh — 2016-01-27T16:59:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-not-to-ct-adrenal-fatigue.5268/post-187468>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/9231409](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/9231409)

Functioning thyroid necessary for cold acclimation?

### omegajosh — 2016-02-12T15:30:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-not-to-ct-adrenal-fatigue.5268/post-188847>

I'd like to withdraw what I said about CTing being automatically bad for people with adrenal issues. I think I was wrong and am happy to say so. For people with AF I think the problem arises from the release of lots of catecholamines from sudden cold exposure e.g. getting into a freezing shower. However, having experiments further with cold baths - I like to give things a fair shot - where I ease myself into them slowly and am able to breathe and control my way through the initial stress response, I haven't experienced the sudden bouts of exhaustion post CT.

Nevertheless, it still takes me close to 2 hours to reall warm back up so that I can think about anything other than being cold!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

### marcus — 2012-12-27T12:25:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-resistance-thin-people.5271/post-68488>

There is a quite scientific question in my mind i just can't get answered... maybe someone can help me, please { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

In Jack's blog "Chapter one on leptinâ€¦" there is a statement that obese people are leptin resistant and thin people as well. I can follow the explanation for obese people but what is going on in thin people? Any ideas? Have I skipped something fundamental?

### Charlotte — 2012-12-27T14:20:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-resistance-thin-people.5271/post-68503>

thank you for bringing it up.

My youngest son is as thin as they ever come. He is 6 foot 6 and weighs maximum 143 pounds. You can see his hip bones from behind. He eats ok, and sometimes even a lot, but he hates fat. When he is home I always have to hide the fat in something. Now he is curbing his sugar intake which used to be huge. Living in Japan, he eats a lot of fish and vegetables, but I am not happy as I see a lot of need for improvement. He is too far away for me to take practial influence. I make him buy coconut oil, butterfat, some supplements and entice him to make cookies from coconut and butter. But something must be wrong with his metabolism.

Could that be Leptin-resistance?

### diane — 2012-12-27T16:23:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-resistance-thin-people.5271/post-68522>

My significant other is somewhat similar. Very skinny and has been his whole life. He's cold a lot - and his favourite food is burgers and fries. He drinks probably 5 cans of Pepsi a day. He could strap on a pair of shoes and run a few miles without being winded, even though he doesn't exercise.

He can go all day without eating, and then eat 2 Big Macs for dinner. But I know it bothers him that he can't gain weight - and even when he does, he doesn't keep it on.

He thinks he's so healthy - and I have absolutely no proof that he's not LOL. But I know that given his habits, he can't be that healthy!Â

### freesia — 2012-12-27T17:05:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-resistance-thin-people.5271/post-68532>

It's unfortunateÂ a couple of people I knowÂ probably won'tÂ consider the possibility of leptin resistance being relevant,Â because they don't pass the "look in the mirror" test. The message they got from one of the blogs is that they need to be overweight.

Anorexia, presumably long term malabsoption (as seen often in my illness),Â or anything that creates inflammation such as gut dysbiosis , heavy metal toxicity....all can contribute toÂ leptin resistanceÂ in the hypothalamus. At least this is the message I got from listening to Dr Kruses earlier "leptin" podcasts! (Highly recommended listening BTW - access them vis the "media" page on this website.)
You can't see most of those things in a mirror.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-27T23:19:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-resistance-thin-people.5271/post-68552>

Anne you are quite correct......LR is not an obese only problem.....it is an energy balance problem and the obese and very thin have that issue in common.

### Lyndra — 2012-12-27T23:28:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-resistance-thin-people.5271/post-68553>

> **Jack Kruse said**
> Anne you are quite correct......LR is not an obese only problem.....it is an energy balance problem and the obese and very thin have that issue in common.

Is that a mitochondrial energy issue?

### marcus — 2012-12-28T01:43:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-resistance-thin-people.5271/post-68557>

Thx for your answers... nonetheless i still don't get the point. How is it possible that thin people can eat the whole day (yes, they even crave for foods) and they don't gain weight.

What is going wrong in their bodies? If they were leptin resistant, wouldn't their metabolism take the same pathway as for obese people (fat cells produce leptin --> receptor in hypothalamus is leptin resistant --> hypothalamus sends "low energy" signal --> cravings for foods --> excess energy/foods while muscle can't burn energy effectively because of LR --> energy is stored). Thus I would expect that thin people gain weight. Where is my mistake?

### marcus — 2012-12-28T01:47:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-resistance-thin-people.5271/post-68558>

> **Anne said**
> ...Â long term malabsoption (as seen often in my illness),Â or anything that creates inflammation such as gut dysbiosis , heavy metal toxicity....all can contribute toÂ leptin resistanceÂ in the hypothalamus.

I can imagine that malabsorption is a cause for thin people. But I can't follow your idea of "malabsorption --> leptin resistance --> no weight gain". Can you explain in more detail, please?

### freesia — 2012-12-28T04:37:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-resistance-thin-people.5271/post-68562>

That's my interpretation of what Jack was saying in his leptin discussions. I don't understand the complexities of leptin/energy balance/any other case by case contributing factors well enoughÂ to give you a concise answer. (Sorry!)Â Maybe a question for Ask Jack if it's not already in the archives? Or perhaps someone else can give a goodÂ explanation?

MyÂ thinkingÂ was... that if leptin resistance is a possibility for an anorexic then it's likely possible for someone malnourished through poor absorption. I'm not sure though if it's an issue for someone who eats all day and can't gain weight.

(Not related to your question, but, for what it's worth, I personally did the leptin rx because a couple of contacts of mine had significant improvement in what they called "adrenal fatigue" symptoms. Not because I needed to lose weight, or gain weight. Poor body composition and inflammation were definite factors in my case.Â Leptin resistance at the receptor level, and high circulating leptin levels due to obesity (causing leptin resistance) aren't the same thing.
Thought I'd give it 30 days...and only needed to 1 to know something was working.)

### Charlotte — 2012-12-28T08:13:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-resistance-thin-people.5271/post-68573>

I am also wondering. Now I am trying to think "loud" by writing it down what I think.

What we know from Dr. Kruses posts is, that leptin resistance and insulin resistance are the both sides of the same coin. I am both, maybe Lino (my son) is only leptin resistant, but not yet insulin resistant. He eats carbs and the insulin is moving it right into the mitochondria where it gets burned into energy. What I noticed is that he has lots of energy, but a nervous kind of energy and it wears off fast.Â  Being leptin resistant makes him eat a lot, but the wrong kinds of food because he craves carbs from eating lots of carbs. So the hypothalamus is faster than the rest of his body by shutting down and making him leptin resistant (after all leptin is not only produced by fat cells or maybe the few he has are sufficient or efficient in producing leptin). Using myself as an example, I still have a lot of fat to lose, but my leptin is almost normal now after 4 months of eating low carb and my body has become more efficient in losing weight. So my son stays skinny by burning his food off, yet, but what if he becomes also insulin resistant? I think he might gain. Not a healthy way though.

I might be totally off track, but I am not a scientist and don't know enough yet, but when he comes home for holidays I will do some experiment with him, like taking his BG and putting him off sugar to low carb and see what happens (if he lets me).

### vkiernan — 2012-12-28T08:23:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-resistance-thin-people.5271/post-68575>

# Thread: Impaired Fasting Glycemia?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/impaired-fasting-glycemia.5272/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Freddieboy — 2012-12-27T12:29:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/impaired-fasting-glycemia.5272/post-68489>

Hi

Hope everyone had a great christmas.

Not sure where to post this, thought here would be good.

So I got given as an early christmas present a blood glucose monitor, a good one, so happy that it's pretty reliable!

The last 4 mornings on waking at 7, after having last eaten at 5 the previous evening, my glucose levels have been 6.4 or 6.5 mmol(115mg/dl).

According to this site and many others I may have Impaired Fasting Glycemia:

[Impaired Fasting Glycemia (IFG) - Impaired Fasting Glucose](http://http://www.diabetes.co.uk/impaired-fasting-glycemia.html)

Looking at the symptoms on the site above, I have had a sort of blurred vision for a while now, have to sort of keep refocusing my eyes if that makes sense. Also I do pee a lot, have high cholesterol and have a fair number of what feel like chest infections coming on, but they dont seem to ever come to much.

I have been low carb for around 3 years now, currently I'm around 100-150g per day, mainly sweet potatoes and fruit. Lots of good fats, butter, coconut milk, avocadoes, EVOO, macadamia nuts, fair amout of fish, beef and lamb once or twice a week.

I exercise around 5 times a week, a mixture of running, cycling and weights, with brisk walks with the dog some days too. Although at present the bikes and runs are all aerobic, no sprinting. So mainly aerobic work with 1, maybe 2 short(15min) weight sessions a week.

Any advice for this? I do intend to get a hospital blood test soon.
Has anyone else had this, and if so what strategies are good to lower the fasting glucose numbers?

Most sites I've read advocate lots of fruit and veg, low fat and lots of grains and fibre!

Many thanks.

### Dali Dula — 2012-12-28T18:21:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/impaired-fasting-glycemia.5272/post-68621>

how are your 2hr post prandial (after meal) readings? 115 is high for fasting blood glucose (FBG). You might want to drop the fruit, low fat, and grains. Read up here on the leptin rx http://www.jackkruse.com/my-leptin-prescription/. You may be one of the lucky ones that recognizes insulin resistance before it becomes Type 2 diabetes and takes measures to prevent it. Good luck.

### Freddieboy — 2012-12-29T09:27:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/impaired-fasting-glycemia.5272/post-68645>

> **Dali Dula said**
> how are your 2hr post prandial (after meal) readings? 115 is high for fasting blood glucose (FBG). You might want to drop the fruit, low fat, and grains. Read up here on the leptin rx http://www.jackkruse.com/my-leptin-prescription/. You may be one of the lucky ones that recognizes insulin resistance before it becomes Type 2 diabetes and takes measures to prevent it. Good luck.

Thanks for reply, I have been checking 1 hr and 2 hrs after meals too, not seen much higher than 115 for that, only once did it get to 130, that was after a meal out for christmas!

I've had a couple of low carb, higher fat days, and the last 2 days it has come down a bit to 109.

I dont have much fruit now, certainly no grains, I just made that comment regarding fruit, low fat and grains as thi be the seems toÂ CW way to treat diabetes.

Cheers

# Thread: Sometimes it's so hard eating even 50 gr of Protein for my BAB!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sometimes-its-so-hard-eating-even-50-gr-of-protein-for-my-bab.5273/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx

### furface — 2012-12-27T16:02:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sometimes-its-so-hard-eating-even-50-gr-of-protein-for-my-bab.5273/post-68518>

I'm not a small guy by any means, and I love my protein, but at times it is so difficult eating even 50 grams of protein...the minimum required, for the Big Ass Breakfast!

This morning for instance, I had a grass-fed lamb burger...25 grams, 5 strips of bacon...17 grams, and 2 scrambled free run omega 3 eggs..12 grams, for a total of 54 grams, and I had to force the last 5 or 6 bites down my throat.

Other mornings, I have no problem eating that much protein, or even more.

Does anyone else ever have a problem eating a minimum of 50 grams of protein every morning?

### diane — 2012-12-27T17:01:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sometimes-its-so-hard-eating-even-50-gr-of-protein-for-my-bab.5273/post-68531>

Yep. Some mornings breakfast just feels disgusting.

I added fat and make bulletproof green tea (I don't do that well with coffee). And will add a half or whole avocado. That makes it much easier and I don't have to get as much protein.

### DarleenMB — 2012-12-27T19:46:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sometimes-its-so-hard-eating-even-50-gr-of-protein-for-my-bab.5273/post-68539>

I have NEVER been able to do 50g of protein. Â I blame it on the fact Jack is, I think I read this somewhere on his blog, 6'5" tall. Â He is a BIG man. Â I'm only 5'9.5". Â I cannot eat that much food in one sitting.

So I make up for it by sucking up the fat. Â I was cooking my bacon in a Nu=Wave cooker which leeches off the bacon fat. Â Now I cook it in the pan so I can keep all that lovely bacon fat and mix it in with either the salmon or the eggs. Â AND I used 1/2 stick of butter in my coffee. Â LOTS of fat for breakfast. Â THAT I can get down with NO problem.

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

I always wonder how someone can have a hard time eating 60 gram of protein. I often eat moose or reindeer and it has 22 grams of protein / 100 grams and I easily eat 1 pound of it.. what makes close to 100 g protein...

\+ quite a lot of added fat.

Salmon has 18,5 g protein / 100 grams and I easily eat 300-400 gram of it in one meal. I rather need to watch for not getting too much protein.

But I eat mostly raw. Maybe cooked is different. I sure feel too stuffed eating cooked eggs I could never get down much protein that way and I almost never eat cooked eggs anyways. Or sausages.. makes me feel like I had a stone in my tummy..lol

I would leave the cooked eggs out.. try some lean fish instead it is not too filling cooked either!

### Charlotte — 2012-12-28T07:27:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sometimes-its-so-hard-eating-even-50-gr-of-protein-for-my-bab.5273/post-68569>

CO in it, plus I add full fat CO milk.Â  I cook my eggs in 1 tbsp. butter and eat 3 slices of bacon.Â  I no longer cook my bacon crispy; I leave it a little fatty.Â

### vkiernan — 2012-12-29T09:29:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sometimes-its-so-hard-eating-even-50-gr-of-protein-for-my-bab.5273/post-68646>

Furface, you will adjust.Â  It took me a bit.Â  I am Inger's size and I don't have a problem at all.Â  I'm sure intitially it was a little tough but I cooked things I enjoyed which made it easier.Â  Your body is going thru a lot right now and it might take a bit of time but it will accommodate it if you continue.Â  Fish is really one of those things that it seems that I have to eat a lot of to feel stuffed so that might be a better option.Â  I truly love my BAB and look forward to it every morning, my favorite time of the day.

### endless — 2012-12-29T11:25:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sometimes-its-so-hard-eating-even-50-gr-of-protein-for-my-bab.5273/post-68654>

I totally agree that raw is easier. I can eat a pound of raw elk easily, but if it was cooked I would have problems. I up the fat, and on days when I just cant face a BAB I will use whey protein and kefir and MCT oil and egg yolks to make a protein drink instead.Â 

I know you said that you arent ready for raw yet...another thing I like is canned salmon fried in bacon fat with chopped bacon and some onion. I have friends in Kitimat that send me home-canned salmon, but any will do! It's really good.

### kathylu — 2012-12-31T16:31:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sometimes-its-so-hard-eating-even-50-gr-of-protein-for-my-bab.5273/post-68853>

I totally sympathize. Eating 50 g protein for breakfast was the absolute hardest thing about the leptin reset for me! I felt nauseous afterwards. It took several weeks of "just do it" before I could. It certainly killed my cravings though. Interestingly, after 8 weeks or so on the reset, you may be able to cut back to a HAB instead of BAB. That's what I was able to do. I only eat 25 to 30 g protein for breakfast now and I am not hungry for 7 to 8 hours afterwards.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-01T12:21:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sometimes-its-so-hard-eating-even-50-gr-of-protein-for-my-bab.5273/post-68900>

> **furface said**
>
> I'm not a small guy by any means, and I love my protein, but at times it is so difficult eating even 50 grams of protein...the minimum required, for the Big Ass Breakfast!
> This morning for instance, I had a grass-fed lamb burger...25 grams, 5 strips of bacon...17 grams, and 2 scrambled free run omega 3 eggs..12 grams, for a total of 54 grams, and I had to force the last 5 or 6 bites down my throat.
> Other mornings, I have no problem eating that much protein, or even more.
> Does anyone else ever have a problem eating a minimum of 50 grams of protein every morning?

Here is something for you all to read regarding protein........and why Rosedale needs to be questioned.

The Role Of Dietary Protein In Optimizing Muscle Mass, Function And Health Outcomes In Older Individuals

Wolfe RR. The role of dietary protein in optimizing muscle mass, function and health outcomes in older individuals. Br J Nutr 2012;108 Suppl 2:S88-93. http://journals.cambridge.org/action/displayAbstract?fromPage=online&aid=8724365

The determination of whether increased dietary protein can positively affect health outcomes is hindered by the absence of prospective, randomized trials directly addressing this issue in which all pertinent variables are controlled. Consequently, we can only address the question deductively by considering the support for the rationale underlying the notion of a beneficial effect of increased dietary protein intake.

With regard to health outcomes, we have focused on older individuals. Muscle mass and function are progressively lost with aging, so that by the age of 60 many individuals have reached a threshold where function begins to be affected. An association between reduced muscle mass and strength and unfavourable health outcomes is more likely to be revealed in individuals who have significant decrements in muscle mass and strength.

In this article support for the rationale underlying the notion of a beneficial effect of increased dietary protein intake is considered. Dietary protein intake, and the resulting increased availability of plasma amino acids, stimulates muscle protein synthesis. If all other variables are controlled, increased muscle protein synthesis leads to improved muscle mass, strength and function over time. Increased muscle mass, strength and function are related to improved health outcomes in older individuals.

Since adverse effects of reasonable increases in protein intake above the recommended dietary allowance (RDA) of 0.8 g protein/kg/day have not been reported, it is reasonable to conclude that the optimal protein intake for an older individual is greater than the RDA.

### kleinb87 — 2013-01-04T16:45:12+0200

The last of the test results are in.

ACTH: 24 pg/ml (6-50)
MRI Brain with and without contrast: Mucosal thickening sphenoid sinus, bilateral maxillary sinuses, ethmoid air cells and frontal sinus with nasal septal deviation noted.
Pituitary Study: No expansion of the sella. No areas of decreased signal are seen on dynamic postcontrast imaging to suggest presence of a pituitary microadenoma. No macroadenoma is identified. No mass effect on the optic chiasm. there is no evidence of invasion of cavernous sinus. Cavernous sinus demonstrates symmetric size, signal and enhancement characteristics. Infundibulum appears to be midline. Pituitary gland measures 3mm in craniocaudal thickness which appears to be somewhat smaller than usually seen in a patient of this stated age and sex.

Consult next week with Dr. Kruse. And hopefully the strings will all be tied together.

### curves64 — 2013-01-11T14:44:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/december-webinar.5276/post-70314>

The QA rocked last night! Dr. K. you gave me two BIG game changers:

Poo Alert.
The first had to do with the constipation I had eating when epi-paleo. I'd never been constipated in my life but over the last 3-4 months I'd become a 1 or 2 on the bristol stool chart. (My poor boyfriend probably should never have opened the door for "poo talk" by playing the Mr. Hanky Christmas Special but that's another story). Anyway, last night you mentioned that constipation might be due to low levels of magnesium so before bed, I popped 3600 mg of Mag. Malate and this morning - voilÃ  - constipation gone!!

Your second game changer came when you talked about CTing and earthing. When I first did the Leptin Reset I CTed in the ocean a lot. Once I started school though, my CT went by the wayside (it's hard to get into the tub when you've experienced CTing in the ocean). When you suggested I walk along the ocean, barefoot, earthing, I realized just how doable that would be. I live only 5 minutes from a beautiful little beach just south of the Golden Gate. A perfect place to CT and earth with my feet in the water at 4 or 5 pm.

Once again Dr. Kruse, thank you for your ever evolving guidance!

P.S. The current class I'm starting is Intro to Biochemistry. I'm hoping that will move me up from being the dummy in the back of the classroom to understanding your blog posts more deeply. { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. # Thread: Can you leave food on your plate?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 24
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Eve — 2012-12-28T16:14:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/post-68610>

I have difficulty stopping eating when I am full. I also have difficulty knowing when I am full. What I currently go by is waiting for the food to not taste as good or it starts to taste bland. However, when it gets to this point and I have a little food left on my plate (e.g., a couple oz of salmon left), I eat it anyway. It typically results in meÂ feeling stuffed.

This morning, I ate about 50g of protein for my BAB. I felt about 80% full afterwards but did not have time to cook some more food as I was already over the 30min mark. Consequently, 2h later I was really hungry.Â  However, other times when I eat 60-100g of protein, I feel too stuffed (esp at 75+).

Yesterday for dinner, I was eating salmon only (due to having leaky gut). I cooked 2 fillets. I felt in my stomach I was full but my brain was telling me to keep eating. I actually felt sad that I was done so soon. So, I cooked up a couple more fillets and ate til I was pretty much stuffed.Â  Actually, this is usually what I do at mealtimes.

How do you know when to stop eating? What is too full?Â

### DarleenMB — 2012-12-28T16:53:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/post-68615>

No help from me. Â Yes I can and do leave food on my plate. Â I don't know what to tell you but when I'm done ... I just know it.

When I was starving myself I couldn't have done that. Â I would eat until I felt I would burst and still feel hungry. Â I think somewhere around 6-7 months into the leptin rx is when I stopped feeling that. Â Mind you I couldn't, and still can't, eat a BAB of 50+g of protein. Â I just can't. Â 

But somehow or other my brain finally has decided I am no longer starving so it knows when to flip that switch.

Maybe you just need more time and MORE FAT!Â  { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. ### PrimalPam — 2012-12-28T17:05:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/post-68617>

I have a hard time leaving food on my plate. For me, it goes back to being raised in a very frugal environment and the frugal habits have carried me throughout my life, in all aspects of life. For example, if I have the tiniest bit of lotion left in a bottle, I'll turn it upside down and scrape the inside of it. Even if I don't particularly like the lotion! I'll just be glad to have used the bottle completely.

So, leaving food on my plate is really hard. In restaurants, I will eyeball my food and decide what portion to take home so I don't overeat. I will then plan which night to eat my leftovers, so as not to waste them.

Now, having said that, it's most definitely easier these days for me to get to that stopping point. When I stay strictly on epiPaleo, eating lots of fat, seafood, and other protein, it's very easy for me to make that switch from "I'm eating" to "I'm done eating".

When I veer off and decide I can eat some carbs, or decide to just "taste one" treat somewhere, my choices go completely out the window. I believe this indicates I still have hormonal and gut issues to work out, so I stay EP as much as I can.

### furface — 2012-12-28T18:32:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/post-68622>

I actually had some food left on my plate yesterday morning during my BAB, when I felt full, but I forced it down my throat to ensure that I got the minimum requirement of 50 gr. of protein. It's funny how much faster I seem to feel full ever since I began the whole Leptin reset, Epi-Paleo diet thing. I also don't miss snacking...and I used to nibble between meals all the time.

One thing to remember though, I believe that we don't have to FINISH our BAB within 30 minutes of rising, just that we have to have at least STARTED to eat our BAB within 30 minutes. Please correct me if I'm mistaken on this. I think it would be virtually physically impossible almost to not only get up, do our morning business, dress (if that's part of what you do), prepare our BAB, and not only start to eat it, but also finish eating all within 30 minutes of rising. I was always taught to chew my food slowly...which I don't do most of the time anyway, but even so, it seems the more appropriate way to eat so that we enjoy our meal, and don't just wolf it down.

### diane — 2012-12-28T23:47:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/post-68634>

I've always had issues with eating until I was full, and then some. Nothing left on the plate.

When I eat the proper BAB (and I've discovered that for me, it's making sure I have enough fat in addition to the protein), I can actually leave food on my plate in subsequent meals. And it's such a weird feeling for me!

If I'm eating carbs, however, so hard for me to stop! When I eat raw, it's even easier to stop!

### Glen PDQ — 2012-12-29T00:14:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/post-68636>

I figure if someone still has a gall bladder, a functioning pancreas, and a normal digestive system then scarfing down larger chunks of meat shouldn't be a problem.Â  I boil bacon (Irish boiled bacon recipe) as part of some meals and tend to chew whole slices just a few times and down the gullet it goes.Â  After I read that meat is digested into a liquid slurry within 20 or 30 minutes I don't worry so much. It is fibrous veggies that probably don't digest well.Â  However, I try not to drink much water during or soon after a meal so that the fat will digest better.

It is wonderful to not be controlled by cravings.

### Charlotte — 2012-12-29T07:08:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/post-68641>

I used to do that also. As long as there was one morsel on my plate, I ate it. I used to check the food on my plate and decide what to eat first. Stupid me ate all I did not like, first and left the best for last, just so I could eat it all. Not even the fact that I observed others doing just the opposite, which is much more clever, because stopping becomes much easier once you get to the things you do not like, I kept doing it my way. I even realized early that enjoying a meal can help to stop earlier, but that did not help either.

My Mom was one of the best cooks I have ever encountered. She started cooking every day after breakfast when we all took off to school and my Dad to work. Coming home around noon, lunch was ready which is THE big meal of the day in Germany. It was always meat and occasional fish, with a yummy sauce, homemade SpÃ¤tzle which is a sort of noddle (making the dough herself and scraping it into boiling water with a knife from a wooden board), dumplings (and they were huge), potatoes or similar carb - rich stuff. Two kinds of vegetables and two kinds of salads. Then of course a dessert. So it was a feast EVERY day and I used to look forward to it from the 3rd or 4th hour into school, hurrying home to get to the dishes. Everybody ate, until our belly buttons turned sideways, my Dad sinking into is wing chair, taking a nap, while we kids rolled off the table to hang out lazy because we were tired from eating. Then, after blood glucose had spiked and come down, we got into the fruit. She had bowls of it around free for the taking. I remember eating 3-4 oranges or apples in one go. Then at 6 it was time for dinner. Another feast of sausages, bread, radishes, another salad, cheeses etc. We were practically eating all day. Of course we were ALL fat. My Mom was the only one who sort of kept her weight in check, but she was fat also, not so much though than us. My Dad had a HUGE belly, I had the biggest ass you have ever seen and my brothers, well they looked like that fattest version of Max and Moritz from Wilhelm Busch's book. And of course my brothers and I fought for the last piece of meat still.

(Once I asked my older brother to hand me the bowl of peas and instead of doing this he put one single pea on my plate. I broke out in tears because I thought that was all I was going to get and I would surely not survive this meal.)

Underneath the message was that this is my mothers way to show her love for her family and we had to appreciate by eating it all. 25 huge dumplings for a family of 5, imagine that and that was only part of the meal. And the plates were empty. We definitely showed our appreciation.

To get beyond all the underlying issues took meÂ  decades.

I cannot tell exactly how it changed, but it took me most of my life, of dieting, gaining, losing (I once fasted for 10 !!! weeks on water and tea alone).Â  It was not an effort, it just sort of happened. Now when I am full, I stop, no matter what is left.

So all I can say is to look at the underlying issues which could be anything, depending on your upbringing, your personal beliefs and fears, such as fear of not getting enough, the belief that nothing should be wasted, still trying to please, (I am just making a few general guesses). Only you can find that out as you are your own collection of experiences which brought you to this place.

### endless — 2012-12-29T11:45:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/post-68658>

> So all I can say is to look at the underlying issues which could be anything, depending on your upbringing, your personal beliefs and fears, such as fear of not getting enough, the belief that nothing should be wasted, still trying to please, (I am just making a few general guesses). Only you can find that out as you are your own collection of experiences which brought you to this place.

BINGO. For me, at least....part of my childhood was spent in 'poverty', on welfare, with plenty of food but not much of it very exciting. treats were rare. I always worried that I wouldn't have enough. If my mom DID make cookies or something, they were strictly rationed. I would sneak into the basement and steal apples, and then feel guilty. I'm still trying to overcome that scarcity that still exists in my psyche. If I am eating clean and staying away from carbs and sugar I am usually able to push the plate away. If not, well, I struggle.

### diane — 2012-12-29T12:47:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/post-68666>

It's interesting - if I eat raw meat or fish, then it's much easier to stop when I'm satisfied. I don't feel "full" - but somehow when I eat raw I don't have the need to feel full like I do when eating other things (especially carbs). Just thought about that as I had raw beef for breakfast.

### caroline — 2012-12-30T02:38:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/post-68723>

That is also how i feel Diane... a little raw meat for breakfast and 2 cups of BFC and I am good to go. I used to be constantly hungry. Â It used to drive me crazy ....now food has no power over me....such a revelation....

### kathylu — 2012-12-31T17:15:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/post-68857>

> **endless said**

BINGO. For me, at least....part of my childhood was spent in 'poverty', on welfare, with plenty of food but not much of it very exciting. treats were rare. I always worried that I wouldn't have enough. If my mom DID make cookies or something, they were strictly rationed. I would sneak into the basement and steal apples, and then feel guilty. I'm still trying to overcome that scarcity that still exists in my psyche. If I am eating clean and staying away from carbs and sugar I am usually able to push the plate away. If not, well, I struggle.

Funny, I've always thought that deprivation had something to do with overeating too. My parents lived through the Depression, when food was really scarce. I remember my mother saying that 6 ounces of meat would be cooked into a stew and served over lots of cheap carbs (bread, noodles) to stretch it enough to feed a family of six. When things got better, it was a point of pride to serve enormous meals, especially if there were guests coming. To show them that you could afford it. And she stockpiled all her life...if something was on sale, she would buy far to much of it "for a rainy day". Things might never be that cheap again! She was never able to break that habit and it took me well into my adulthood to leave it behind.

### Inger — 2013-01-02T03:03:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/post-68974>

My own Nr1 is that raw fats might be the last inflammatory food.. IDK if it has anything to do with reality but I feel they are just so healing. ### Inger — 2013-01-02T14:05:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/post-69026>

Caroline, at nite.. I often read too!
I have only candles or oil lamps in my home so it is dark here but I do often use my computer. I like to do the other stuff when it is light outside, and then relax in the evening. I use f.lux, turn the screen at lowest possible light settings (wich are still way too light for my liking) and put dark-brown sunglasses on, And often my new hat.

I use my leptop and when I bend the screen far away it looks darker too so I do that also, especially as it goes later. But I usually get in bed at 9:30 PM or at 10 PM. Later is just a struggle.. I get so tired!
Sometimes I dance to some nice music. Or sit down in front of the big mirror and keep companionship to myself and talk..lol When all that happends in this house grows over my head. I always feel good again when I look at me, it is so weird. I guess I am my good friend. Maybe I am crazy too.

Sometimes I am downstairs talking to family. Here are quite often guests too so I often help my mom then to serve them.
Sometimes I prepair some foods in candle light for the next day like marinated herrings or stuff.
Sometimes I have to work.
In summer I love to spend the evening outside.. as it is so long light here! I love it. Or I sit at my balcony and enjoy the magic sunset. I am not being much inside at all in summer. It is 1000 times more healthy to stay outside I am sure. It might be some mold in this house so that is a good reason to spend the summer hours as little as possible in here to get some break from mold too.

If I eat too late and drink wine my sleep gets disturbed too so I avoid that. I drink wine at daytime now, when I seldom have some. No rules without exceptions though..lol
Like now at Christmas I couple of times had a glass of wine in the evening my bro or dad offered. It was kind of sweet, not organic.. it made me feel not so good. I am very picky with my wine.

If I were you, and my computer gave me sleep issues, I would be outside.. you could pick seaweeds or mussels at the beach.. or look at the sunset. Or if it is dark, make some art! I love to be creative.. you can do painting or photography or knit or write or.. anything!
I need to be less on the computer too but I learn so much through it. I use it only for learning and things that are of high value to me though.

### Inger — 2013-01-02T14:19:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/post-69030>

I seldom leave anything on my plate.. I mostly eat the same amounts and prepaire the same amounts and if I am still hungry I eat some more!

I am doing it the totally wrong way I know! If I eat cooked I never cook too much mecause I never reheat anything. I feel no good from reheated foods. But as I mostly eat raw it is no issues with leftovers, I just eat until full and the rest keeps wonderful for next meal. I have a weird habit of not eating on a plate always also. Like oysters. I just shuck them on after one and as long as I like it. Or cheese, like roquefort. I just slice off pieces and eat until satisfied. Or if I have a slab of raw meat in my fridge I slice thin slices off and eat, I do it until I feel I had enough.
When preparing fish I know how much I eat. It is always around 400 grams.
For dinner I just had;

5 big oysters, raw and shucked by me, for a starter, then,
300 gram lowfat wildcaught fish cooked for a minute and then I added 100 g precooked mussels and some spoonful of EVOO,
then for dessert I had a small portion of grassfed lamb cooked with organic cabbage I had made for family. I love lamb with cabbage and I always have to taste some when I make it even if I prefer my meat raw. I do the fatty raw lamb in my dehydrator at body temp in the evening and eat it warm and fatty for breakfast. Makes me feel in heaven!
Then I had 2 glasses of seaweed water. HA quite a mix one could think but now I feel just GREAT.

### Charlotte — 2013-01-02T14:39:56+0200

Inger, I think I have to think about dessert again. To me that sounded like a 3-course-meal, but without dessert.

### Inger — 2013-01-02T15:53:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/post-69041>

Don't get me started Charlotte...! Dessert.. as you mentioned in the CT thread.. if I really want something I make me too..lol and like you, desserts with no regrets..

> > Leaving food on your plate become natural when you eat well. All food is inflammatory.......but some are less inflammatory than others.......our goal is to limit that inflammation and optimize our hormone panels.
>
> Dr. Kruse ..... coud you expand on this a little more please? I have been thinking about this for a long time. How do we know what is more inflammatory than something else? I realize some things are going to be very obvious - but I think I could be doing a much better job if I knew more. I decided to stop eating eggs just to see....I Think I do much better without them ..... but I don't really know for sure or why. What should we be looking for? What is the least inflammatory food? or is that different for everyone? I feel - for me - raw is by far and away the best .....no digestion problems what so ever and I eat way less and feel lighter. So is this accurate for me? or just something I am assuming??? Are there ways to tell if our inflammation is coming down .... other than testing? Are there telltale markers?

The answer was in the December webinar.........leakiness at the first cytochorme determines how inflammatory a food is........the cruel part of life is that it needs water and electrons from food to sustain it.......but the same Rx for life slowly kills it because of how it is constructed. We are not designed to live forever for a good reason........Biology requires replacement.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-leave-food-on-your-plate.5277/post-71832>

Inger I hope your stress resolves soon. Glad to see that you are able to enjoy your food in peace and that your family supports you in your food choices. Nice!
