### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-01-08T23:09:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epcotx-is-now-live.5393/post-70009>

Ok, I finally got my period since July. Just needed to tell everyone. Don't know if I even like it though b/c of the bad symptoms. It's not worth it if I eat so well and still get bad symptoms. I can try to convince myself that it's a. after so many months it's the hormones sorting itself out and it is a bit 'messy' in the process b. 'detox'

That's what I thought last year at this time (not regarding lack of menses after a time) but for signs of detox etc. I thought I was on a road to optimal...but I surely hit a major road block somewhere that I am not sure where/when it happened.

Onions...ya!

### ashryn — 2013-01-09T01:20:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epcotx-is-now-live.5393/post-70020>

> **Zorica Vuletic said**
>
> Ok, I finally got my period since July. Just needed to tell everyone. Don't know if I even like it though b/c of the bad symptoms. It's not worth it if I eat so well and still get bad symptoms. I can try to convince myself that it's a. after so many months it's the hormones sorting itself out and it is a bit 'messy' in the process b. 'detox'
> That's what I thought last year at this time (not regarding lack of menses after a time) but for signs of detox etc. I thought I was on a road to optimal...but I surely hit a major road block somewhere that I am not sure where/when it happened.
> Onions...ya!

Zorica, give it a chance to work! It is hardly surprising that the first Px after 6 months is difficult.. (Though I really feel for you, I know all about the difficult part - hang in there lady)

That it has come back is a sign that things are normalising, but it seems there is a lot involved with hormones because they are all so dependant on each other, and the body needs to work out which ones to spit out at what time depending on what else is floating around in your system...

Also.. As the good Doc says, Its hard to know whats going on with \*you\* unless you have labs to compare all the dependant systems, so no one can really guess at your best treatment because everyone is different. Unless you get them and find someone who can decipher them, you're only ever going to have a trial and error approach, and some lucky people will hit the right things straight away, and some won't because it isn't always easy to see the big picture..

I don't know if it will help you, but strapping a flat bag of ice to my belly for a couple of hours each day REALLY helped me.. I mean really really.. Last ToM, I took no painkillers at all and it only lasted 4.5 days instead of 6. If you give it a go, i'd love to know if it works for you too... All these years I have used hot baths and heat packs, when really it was cold I needed.

Hope you feel better soon - don't give up!

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-01-11T19:30:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epcotx-is-now-live.5393/post-70352>

Hi there. I haven't checked this thread in 4 days. I should have put ice packs on my abdomen, of course! I did use this technique in the Spring and it did help a lot, you're right.

I can't gaurentee that mine will be shorter at least for this month. I am hoping two things for next month:

1\. That it comes again (And within a nice 28-30 day period)
2\. I do not suffer bad PMS like this month again.

I will continue taking Vitex. In July I took Vitex and it returned then, but I stopped since I felt 'sooo good' and with the new introduction of all the seafood I thought just diet alone could be enough. This time I will stick with the Vitex for a longer period of time just to see the difference. If my cycle becomes more regular etc.

Thanks again for your kind words.

### Charlotte — 2013-01-12T00:43:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epcotx-is-now-live.5393/post-70374>

Hi Zorica,
I used to have really bad cramps with my period every month. Really bad to where I could only lie down, take medication and wait for it to go away. When I moved to America and lived in the wilderness I had no medication anymore which my dad used to give me for these cramps.
So for half a year I suffered through this month by month having nothing to ease the pain.
One day, I had been lying again for two days in pain, I got really fed up with it, I just had it and I decided I did not want this any more and not knowing any better (I did not know about paleo, leptin reset or any like that) I got up, put my boots on and started hiking straight up a mountain through the snow. Every step was hard but I kept pushing and I climbed for two hrs, absolutely relentlessly. When I got to the top of the mountain, my pain was gone and from that day on, I never had any pain with my period ever again. It came every 28 days with a few drops and left as quietly as it has come. I do not know what I actually did or what happened, but wading through that snow at the fastest pace I could, pushing my way up to the top of the mountain did it.
For me, that was a key experience which started with my decision and will to be done with that. What happened then I do not know. I guess the other was instinct.

Reading Ashryns post I guess it was the cold as my feet got really wet and cold as I was pushing through the snow. I think it could work for you too.

### diane — 2013-01-12T12:14:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epcotx-is-now-live.5393/post-70396>

I had painful cramps as well, and they started as I turned 40. Somehow, starting in September, I now have no pain, very minimal PMS symptoms (bad moods, although still have slight cravings), although my periods have gotten longer and heavier, which I think will sort itself out as well. I have been trying to figure out what caused the change, and the key things I did was start turning out the lights/blue blockers, started DHEA (which I have stopped because I ran out but this hasn't turned the period back to the way it was) and way more seafood. And CT, although I didn't do as much as some people do. It may be a combination of all of them. Now to figure out what will normalize my cycle.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-01-12T14:10:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epcotx-is-now-live.5393/post-70417>

Thanks everyone! I really hope that it was rough this month because it was the first cycle in 6 months. That's quite a long time to go for someone who is 27 (I think). So hopefully it was akin to when someone starts taking Progesterone and it actually makes things 'worse' for the first bit but then gets levelled out and things dramatically improve. I really HOPE that is my case!

Hmmm Charlotte, the hiking and the cold...interesting. I don't know about what Jack says but I know that when I exercise which really hasn't been at all lately (I DO feel much much better). I think I should add in some exercise of some sort like sprints or weight training. I don't 'think' my hypothalamus is so messed up that I can't exercise. I would just make sure I don't over do it. This doesn't mean I don't walk alot though...I walk pretty much every where.

\*I'm pretty sure I over did it when I was in karate only because it was so addicting. I basically went 5 or 6 times a week (Jan to Aug. 2011). MASSIVE SHITSTORM FOLLOWED and/or some kind of crash. I feel like in some ways I am still 'making' up from that period in time...sigh. O well. I did have I guess what you call a crash in 2002 (didn't realize it at the time though) and I felt like I had a bit smaller ones throughout university. But 2002 and 2011 were the two biggest ones. Probably I say 2011 was the worst!! I say that because it felt like additional PTSD or something on top. It was very horrible and I'm very glad I do not have to worry about that again. I might complain but when I complain it is nothing like that last half of that year; thank god!

I find for day 4 and 5 I still get a bit of lower abdomen cramping around lunch time. It goes away though not too long after.
I also feel like my body is more on the constipated side again...even though I'm taking ox bile 500mg. It's not as bad but stools are hard to pass again, and it feels like it's getting stuck more in the lower part of the colon. I also always struggle with a puffy tongue no matter what. Same with puffy under the eyes. Really wish I could get rid of it. I even stopped coffee at the beginning of this month too... I think for now it's a good idea to avoid coffee just so that it doesn't give me too much adrenaline rushes. It can feel good but it only makes me more tired afterwards.

I also have Bragg apple cider vinegar. I started up taking the regular ACV yesterday but thought maybe the Braggs is better with the mother etc.

My eyes have been feeling much clearer lately which is good but I do feel a bit light headed at times. I think that's still better than feeling fuzzy and foggy in the head.

We'll see how tomorrow is. lol

By the way, yes it would be so nice if POOF PMS sorts of stuff just magically vanishes without much else changing. In July when my period came after 3 months (I took Vitex for that month) it was bad PMS leading up to it, but the actual period itself had like zero cramping and it was shortened a day from 7 to 6. That's why I thought I was in the clear... so I will stick with the Vitex for a few months and see how the next few cycles go for me. Maybe on the actual day I will hike vigorously no matter how I feel---and February is a nice cold month.

Thanks again.

### vkiernan — 2013-01-12T15:12:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epcotx-is-now-live.5393/post-70425>

Hi Zorica. You have mentioned the puffy tongue, eyes, constipation, bad mood and lethargy quite a bit in all of your posts. From what I have read, all of these can point to a thyroid problem. I am wondering if you have a little adrenal fatigue going on from your crazy workouts which in turn has tanked your hormone panel and thus why you have no periods on schedule. Read the hormone 101 blog. You could try magnesium citrate to help you move your bowels. You need to make sure you are able to get those toxins out of your body. Have you tried making your own sauerkraut and kombucha for the gut? Pretty easy to do and this might help you keep things moving.

You are young, I would think if you stay the course and do all of the Rx's Jack has mentioned that things will right themselves. But, this will take time. I'm not talking a month or two either. Although, since you are young you might see bennies way faster than some of us who could be your mother. I would bet too that getting your circadian rhythm in line will help you immensely.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-01-12T15:42:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epcotx-is-now-live.5393/post-70426>

Thanks V! I have been working with these protocols since last January. I might have made some unknowing errors\* here and there which could have slowed my progress overall, but it in relation to the adrenal stuff that was probably longer to recover. Especially during the summer I really benefitted from perfect circadian timing ie. more light. So on a whole year basis I think there has been more progress than not, just have been having trouble in the last few months. I will stay the course for sure and I do feel better than last month if I compare date for date.

\*In June I think I did not help myself for adrenal wise, but only by accident. It did not lead to any crashes or anything thankfully I just think made a hiccup in my process.

I eat sourkraut and occasionally kombucha (expensive so I need to get the mother culture or something to make my own for way cheaper). I eat seaweed for all meals now starting this month. Before I ate plenty of seaweed but not 3 x /day.

I was first thinking about thyroid last month but I was reading about adrenal fatigue etc and I decided that thyroid is not my primary concern. I would like to avoid needing any thyroid meds so instead I am focusing on gut (but I thought I was doing that before) and now liver. I think the ox bile does help even if I am saying that I might feel more constipated than not. When I eat with the ox bile I feel normal satiated instead of overly full. It feels more comfortable.

I will be patient and kind to my body.

I will do all the right things necessary for good health and when the time comes that I can afford testing I can either check to see how much progress I've made or if I still have concerns, then I can then zero in better. I figure though at least in the meantime at least I can do as much to help myself out anyway. I don't need to be able to afford tests in order to do the right thing---Inger does it, so then I can too.

I must admit though I do miss coffee... I don't feel like I crave it but I really enjoyed a nice steaming cup of coffee from some specialty origin. Jamaica Blue Mountain and Cuban Red Mountain are two very good ones that are not in just any store, even health organic ones even. Often you need to go to the country and/or have family send them to you. So it makes it extra special. But for now I am liking that I stay away from coffee (at least for now).

Another question: Do you think that after some time with more regular menses that the thyroid should begin to behave better? Also, for waking up and looking at the sun: It rises so late like almost 8am. I often cannot sleep that late. If it's still dark for another hour, should I wait until the sun to look at it and then eat, or would it be OK to eat and then look at the sun. I'm always confused about this. In the summer it's of course easy to wake up look at the sun and then eat....not in winter. In the Fall I would wake around 6am eat then look at the sun. I felt like the loss of daylight really affected me (which I thought would not have this time around given all the seafood etc....hmmm).

Thanks!

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-01-12T15:48:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epcotx-is-now-live.5393/post-70428>

p.s. The crazy karate routine was only 8 months and it's been about a year and a half. Also in 2002 when I had a crash I was not doing ANY exercise regimen at all. I think I am just prone to adrenal and/or hypothalamic stress. So I just have to be more careful...It's also probably why I respond so well to proper circadian cycles (and also why so sensitive to adverse affects too). At least I don't work night shift and I never will. Money is simply not THAT important to basically, 'kill' myself. I would not tolerate it at all. (As if anyone can, really)...

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-01-12T16:14:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epcotx-is-now-live.5393/post-70431>

Of course it's all tied in some way or another. So I don't know if thyroid makes for adrenal stress etc. I just hope if I am super good to my liver (as well as gut) that things can sort themselves out and that I don't need thyroid meds b/c dr.s won't help me due to my TSH being 'OK' (ish). It did rise from Oct. to Nov. Does that indicate Hashi's at all? I don't have any other T3 or T4 related tests, never mind free T3 etc. It went from 1.7 something to 2.38. I might test again just to see at least that part.

Although I know TSH only measures what the pituitary is doing---that's why I hope that with normalization of my cycle that the thyroid will sort itself out. \*cross fingers\*

By the way I've always had issues with 'dryness' etc. and puffiness. As a teen ew my face looked more bloated than it is now. I just saw a pic from an ID card I have of a picture from when I was about 17 and I noticed similar puffiness and darkness under the eyes. (Glasses off in pic). Ew.

So ya...liver. Do your job!!

# Thread: sleep disorders and vitamin D leve;s

### Holly — 2013-01-08T21:08:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-disorders-and-vitamin-d-leve-s.5395/post-69977>

Very interesting lecture by a neurologist regarding sleep disorders and vitamin D levels. (good speaker and easy to listen to)
I wonder if Dr Kruse would agree? Has anyone heard of her? \[video=youtube\_share;h7cbBB1c0IM\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?feature=player\_embedded&v=h7cbBB1c0IM\[/video\]

### Glen PDQ — 2013-01-09T23:53:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-disorders-and-vitamin-d-leve-s.5395/post-70120>

According to her youtube video Dr. Gominak suggests we live in a permanent winter because we stay indoors most of the time which creates a vitamin D deficiency from lack of UVB bombarding our skin. Contrast this with Dr. Kruse' observation that most people live in a permanent summer due to the abundant starch/sugar/carbohydrates as food. Both descriptions work and are not contradictory.

Dr Gominak observed that the only vitamin D receptors in the brain are in the area that causes REM sleep paralysis and that Growth Hormone is released as we cycle through phases of REM sleep. This suggests our bodies can't prevent or heal disease if it lacks sufficent Hormone D and adequate deep sleep.

### caroline — 2013-01-10T00:17:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-disorders-and-vitamin-d-leve-s.5395/post-70121>

I have had very low Vitamin D for years and I live in Australia. When my Docs said to go outside more - it never made sense. I am outside every single day - so - How can that be? They never had an answer - even supplementing with Vit D didn't help much if any. Now that Dr. K. is helping me get my hormones and light cycles and sleep on track - my Vit D has come up tremendously. I am wondering if there is much difference between different brands of Vit D?

### Huck — 2013-01-10T09:33:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-disorders-and-vitamin-d-leve-s.5395/post-70146>

>
> I have had very low Vitamin D for years and I live in Australia. When my Docs said to go outside more - it never made sense. I am outside every single day - so - How can that be? They never had an answer - even supplementing with Vit D didn't help much if any. Now that Dr. K. is helping me get my hormones and light cycles and sleep on track - my Vit D has come up tremendously. I am wondering if there is much difference between different brands of Vit D?

I believe when vitamin d is in a huge deficit, sunlight alone is usually not enough to replenish it. I had to get a vitamin d shot, supplement at 15,000 IU/day, and get a couple of hours of sun most days during the summer to finally get my vitamin d into upper range.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-10T15:45:56+0200

I was doing 10,000 iu a day.I started progesterone about 3 months ago and pregnenolone and DHEA. My Vit D went from 45 to 149 in that time. JK said my D had been so low because my hormones were in the tank.

### Glen PDQ — 2013-01-11T00:21:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-disorders-and-vitamin-d-leve-s.5395/post-70262>

D3 is supposed to be the preferred form for humans, not D2 which is plant-based if I recall. I believe almost all D3 supplements are sourced from the lanolin on raw wool. Livestrong says D3 is activated by liver and kidney enzymes. Maybe those with liver or kidney problems can't activate D3 adequately?

### Patty Cakes — 2013-01-11T20:29:04+0200

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-disorders-and-vitamin-d-leve-s.5395/post-70448>

I think of course magnesium plays a role here too...well all vits and micro nutrients do...

Things keep pointing to the liver (for me anyways) no matter what angle I look at whether from hormones like Pg/E or if it's thyroid. Well I don't mean to override the hypothalamus as the master, but the liver is pretty important. Also in regards to cellular efficiency and mitochondria again a good working liver helps with this majorly.

If I had the balls (or ovaries for women?) to test using liposomal glutothione and huge liver focused regimen of supplements and eat all crap and drink alcohol non stop it would be interesting to see how long it would 'work'. See I wouldn't want to do this to myself but it would be cool if someone was willing to do this to themselves---Jack? Do you want to try this? lol

Liver. Mitochondria. Hypothalamus.

### Kiera2 — 2013-01-14T06:46:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-disorders-and-vitamin-d-leve-s.5395/post-70643>

Here in Australia our reference range is 75-150nmol/L. I tested after supplementing for a few months (dumb, I know. TEST FIRST), and my Vit D was 349. Which would be 140ng/ml, and I felt fantastic.

### Glen PDQ — 2013-01-14T12:36:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-disorders-and-vitamin-d-leve-s.5395/post-70670>

The Vitamin D Council list the following cofactors in order of importance:

Magnesium
Vitamin K
Vitamin A
Zinc
Boron

His type of British understatement and dry humor is always great and a lot of the comments are useful too. I go to Dr. Deans' blog pretty often--I use it as an index to other paleo/nutrition blogs that I like.

But I also get tired of reading just this stuff and want something different, so I also check out The Browser most days -- it aggregates interesting writing from lots of different publications.

I want to try to limit my online time so I can unplug my router for stretches of of time during the day. Also trying to remember to unplug before I go to bed at night. Not much traction on either goals yet, but I'm going to keep trying.

### MamaGrok — 2013-01-09T16:57:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-70087>

What I see is

\*great improvement in hematocrit, etc., part of CBC
\*Despite the fact that every increase in Naturethroid is making me feel better (with possible exception of the last two ... on reflection, I think my mood has improved, but energy has been down the last month), I have too much pooling. ReverseT3 is not yet in, so no idea if all the extra T4 is going to that or not.
\*LDL is still being converted so something, so that's good
\*HDL is decreasing despite a year with no wheat or dairy. Could something else have been masking it before? Kruse says low HDL is normal with leaky gut.
\*Iron labs - yikes. How are these going down while the ones in CBC are going up?
\*SHBG & pregnenolone still royally suck. What to do? Supp DHEA even though my DHEA is better than ever?
\*Estrogen is up but progesterone is not. Why? Note the 1st & 3rd were taken around CD5, while the 2nd was taken post-ovulation
\*I have no idea what the CD4:CD8 or ACTH levels indicate.
\*Still no positive or negative confirmation of Hashimoto's or other autoimmune disease
\*Why is CMP going downhill? What does the BUN/Creatinine thing indicate?
\*What is happening with my Sodium, Potassium, & Chloride?
\*Do I need to do something about my Ferritin?

You can see the labs in a much more readable format here: http://www.marksdailyapple.com/forum/thread4753-119.html#post1056602

### Destiny — 2013-01-10T07:25:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-70136>

I believe that the BUN/Creatinine ratio reflects how your kidney function. It can also point to upper gastrointestinal bleeding.

When my ferritin was low I supplemented with highly absorbable iron and vitamin c.

### Shijin13 — 2013-01-10T09:47:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-70147>

low BUN/Creatine = mitochondrial inefficiencies. (learned that recently in a consult and in the Dec webinar!)

as for Pg:E2 ratio - you want to get that around CD 21. CD3 or 4 gives a picture of where your hormones are at the beginning of the cycle...

here's a good description of what ACTH can show - depending on the test performed....
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adrenocorticotropic\_hormone
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ACTH\_stimulation\_test

if your SBGH and Pregnenelone are both in the toilet - you probably need to consider supplementing w/both Pregnenolone and dhea. I like LEF pregnenlone - I know JanSz likes nutracetuicles

maybe start w/25mg of pregnenolone + what DHEA your taking...

I'd say your gut issues - as a result of HASHIs is impacting your LDL conversion b/c of low T3. - but you know this... I'd guess your body is just idiling...one thing w/ the lower mid day cortisol you might want to consider is having a small midday meal to help balance out the cortisol... say smoked oysters, or 10 shrimp... something just to help balance your cortisol so it doesn't take a dive...

### MamaGrok — 2013-01-10T09:49:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-70148>

Thanks for the reply! That's exactly my concern - all signs of GI bleeding have been gone for a year now (had a couple bouts of tarry stools that were very concerning), and the "waking up with an hour of labor-like waves of gut pain followed by round upon round of diarrhea" has disappeared ever since I started taking ox bile last fall. So I don't understand why BUN/creatinine is getting worse instead of better.

And my ferritin is through the roof. Yikes! I know it means inflammation, since it clearly doesn't mean the opposite of anemia, given the context of my other labs, so the question here, too, is why is this getting worse instead of better?

### MamaGrok — 2013-01-10T09:55:43+0200

I'd guess your body is just idiling...one thing w/ the lower mid day cortisol you might want to consider is having a small midday meal to help balance out the cortisol... say smoked oysters, or 10 shrimp... something just to help balance your cortisol so it doesn't take a dive...

Thanks! So what does high BUN/Creatinine, out the roof, mean? That's where I am, worse every time.

I have a decent feel for what ACTH indicates; I just don't know the ranges for optimal.

I know the protocol Jansz has shared is to supp DHEA until it has done all it can do for Pregnenolone, and then supp pregnenolone directly ... but I'm wondering if there's any reason to bother with DHEA at all, since my DHEA levels are in the optimal range, at least according to STTM which is usually very accurate.

I read several places to do 5mg of DHEA to start with, and that most will end up around 10-15mg. The supp I bought is 100mg caps! When I took them for close to a week, I couldn't sleep, lol. I wonder if I can break them open.

My SHBG isn't in the toilet; it's out the top of the range through the roof!

I need to re-do the cortisol. That's from last year, and I was having lunch at that time, or all the way up until maybe a few weeks before the test. Right now, I'm doing a circadian T3 protocol, taking 1 grain 2hrs before I wake up. It is doing wonders for how I feel in the morning. Yay!

### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-10T11:46:05+0200

Jan webinar will get into the BUN creat thing.......it means your dehydrated and as H2O is an exhaust of mitochondrial ATP production it tells us you have more energy efficiency to find.......for some this is bad news but from my perspecitive it means you can get even better.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-10T11:47:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-70158>

SHBG is a function of many things......it should come down as your T3 and circadian signaling improve. If not then you have to worry about hormones and body comp......but your fat loss has helped you......it will rise when your PG/E2 is way off........and yours maybe because of your current changes over the last 18 months

### MamaGrok — 2013-01-10T11:48:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-70159>

I'm planning on another konsult after the rest of my labs come in (reverse T3, nutrient analysis, 23andme, enterolabs), but for now, I really need to figure out what to do about my thyroid dose.

I take

\-1 grain Naturethroid 2hrs before waking
\- 1 grain on waking
\- 1 thyrogold at noon (said to be 2.6 grains)
\- 2 grains NT at about 2PM

I did the stopthethyroidmadness protocol of starting at 1/2 grain per day, and increasing every two weeks so long as each increase made me feel better and not hyper. That's how I got to where I am. But in the last 3 weeks or so, I feel increasingly better mood, but less energy, and it appears T3 is pooling.

### MamaGrok — 2013-01-10T12:03:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-70162>

Oops, just saw the last replies. Thank you! Between green tea, water w/ ACV, plain water, & broth, I drink a \*ton\*. But I notice that when I eat something that disagrees with me, even the smallest amount, I retain water all over, up to 6 lbs in a day, and then, 6 days later, I will pee a ton and it will be gone the next day. But I was clean and had only the slightest amount of bloating & water retention when I had these blood tests run. ?

There's the PHD explanation - I have dry eyes & mucus membranes b/c I'm low carb long-term - but it doesn't feel right to me.

### caroline — 2013-01-10T15:40:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-70192>

I don't know if this is any help at all - but I am finding I am much more sensitive to things I eat now. I retain huge amounts of fluid if I eat smoked salmon or canned anything and I think bacon too. I seem to need to eat very simply - just some raw meat and raw seafood .....even shrimp seems to be a problem - I will retain fluid after shrimp too. ### Patty Cakes — 2013-01-10T19:03:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-70217>

Did I read that right: you are high in both FT3 and FT4? TSH will be suppressed when taking NDT so that doesn't look that bad to me. I'd say you are on too many meds. I know you have said you feel like you could use more but I wonder if the fatigue is from the adrenals. If you have poor cortisol levels and you could be experiencing pregnenolone steal (very low level). Plus your DHEA seems a little low to me. You want to be in the top of the range. I got mine up through supplementation to 350 and was told that was great and I should keep it there.

Back to thyroid, I bet you do have Hashis but because your diet is so tight the antigens that would irritate/stimulate the antibodies are not present thus keeping your ABs down. This is good if you have Hashis.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-01-10T19:37:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-70224>

Stay on that CR morning NDT dose. That protocol completely cleaned up my adrenals. I was still doing the sun exposure in the morning, orange glasses at night, and CT so there was lots of support for those adrenals.

My DHEA level started around 160 and Dr. K told me to take the 100mg pills. I did and it took a few months to get it to 350. I stopped taking that much because I didn't want to go higher but then the level dropped so I take it a couple times a week. Going to test again soon.

According to Dr. K you need to keep testing those ABs for 18 months. But what if it keeps telling you the same thing? Based on your labs I would bet you have it. My girlfriend was diagnosed about the same time I was. She was 45 and post-menopausal. Her ABs were around 200. Now four years later she has cleaned up her diet and her ABs look like yours do and she had to drop her meds way down. Her food was aggravating the ABs and her thyroid didn't like it. Finding out won't really mean anything except for the food that you can eat. I want to do the Enterolabs test because I miss tomatoes, whip cream, and peppers! I do eat some nuts still but not many at all. But the longer I eat this way the more I get used to it.

I'm so surprised about the Thyro-Gold. I started to add it into my day after reading what great luck you are having on it. I subbed out the T3 afternoon dose with 1 tablet of TG. I did it for three days and couldn't keep it up any longer! I was so tired and exhausted within four hours of taking it. Not sure why unless there is a filler I was reacting to.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-01-11T20:03:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-70354>

MamaGrok, I started T3 only in March '12. I went from 2 1/2 grains of NDT to starting T3 dose of 12.5 in the morning and 6.25 taken in the afternoon. I took temps, HR, and BP reading throughout the day. For me when my BP went up I knew it was time for a dose. I increased slowly based on how I felt and my readings. I worked my way up to 52mcg in three doses and felt good but not tip top after four months of T3 only. Was afraid to go much higher but in my consult with Jack he said I should feel a lot better so I got the doses up to 25mcg 3x/day. This was on a Turkish brand of T3 called Tiromel. I'm told it is much weaker than Cytomel USA brand but my experience is not that at all. I am currently on Cytomel and the dose has stayed the same for me. Most of how I base my dose is on energy.

As far as the morning dose I started at 2 hours prior to wake up. I moved it back even further to three hours, in half hour increments, but didn't feel as well in the morning so the perfect time for me was that two hours prior to waking time.

I never "felt" my rT3 clear but it did based on my labs in August. I also did a saliva ASI test at that time and my adrenals were basically in the proper place for the four time periods (STTM recommendations). Almost perfect! I felt great too. Now five months later I have some Italian T3 and have dropped my dosage down to 62mcg and feel the same...great! I changed my morning dose to 'upon wake up' in Nov and have kept it that way ever since. I really want to go back on NDT but I also feel so great that I don't want to change!

Also wanted to say to you that I feel this incredible burst of fabulous energy and well being shortly have taking D-Ribose. I went out and got some after I had a friend tell me she loves it and the Dec webinar. I'm wondering if it will make you feel more energized too!

I wish I could better navigate the new website too.

A good read that seems to be right up the alley of optimizing mitochondrial function is, "Fatigued to Fantastic." I started taking d-ribose at the recommended dose from the book (15g/day), along with acetyl-l-carnitine at LEF doses (2000-3000mg/day). I think I can say pretty confidently that I am feeling a difference with these supplements. I have also been taking ubiquinol, but I think I need to increase the dose. I think it has also helped my blood pressure (used to run really low like yours), which would imply adrenal function. I feel more stable, less deathly in the mornings.

Also, I am most intrigued that you hav noticed a weight gain when you eat something that doesn't agree. That is my experience as well. About 6 weeks ago I trialed white rice (1/2 cup serving), and it wrecked my week. Bloating and pressure the night I ate it, then stomach upset the next day and weight gain. Then joint pain, overall achiness, even a little sciatica for the next few days. Finally, 6 days later, symptoms disappeared, I dropped the weight, and I started feeling better. I did the experiment to figure out if rice powder in my supplements was a problem. I got my answer loud and clear! As you might remember, the list of foods that cause weight gain (water retention) for me is a long list. I had really thought the white rice would be "okay." I ate it with digestive enzymes on board too.

> positive - your only seafood sensitivity is tuna.... seafood is game on!

Sadly, no. The only seafood they test for is tuna. So I could be sensitive to \*all\* seafood, or I could be more sensitive to salmon than tuna, or cod or sole or shrimp or oysters or ..... I just don't know.

So if I take out tuna and increase some other seafood, with the knowledge I currently have, I could be doing myself as much harm as good. I just don't know at this point.

I lowered my NDT the last three days, down to 1 grain 2hrs before waking, 1 grain on waking, and two in the afternoon. Still feeling good.

I've always loved the irony that I get hyper swings with Hashi's and possibly hyper with over meds, but never the weight loss, lol. I did have some shakiness a few days ago, and fast pulse, so I'm pretty sure I needed to decrease it some. Hopefully not too much, but the easiest thing to do was to drop the Thyrogold.

### bigknitwit — 2013-01-15T18:25:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-70927>

All I can add here is that you may consider doing an elimination diet, and then trial and error to figure out the seafood issues. There was a segment on Dr. Oz (gasp), when he interviewed a woman who wrote a book called "The Plan." She hypothesized that most people gain weight because they continue to eat foods to which they are sensitive. She works with people to figure out their individual sensitivities. Her plan includes a 3 day "cleanse." Here she has a menu the includes eating foods she has found to be the least sensitive for the most people. Then on the 3rd or 4th day, foods are added back in, one by one every other day. She says you know you are sensitive to a food when you get bloating/gas/discomfort upon eating it, followed by weight gain the following day (0.5 to 2 pounds). This appealed to me, because I always thought my food problems started following an elimination diet for the sake of my nursling 2 years ago. When I added foods back in, my weight kept going up and up 1-2 pounds each day. Over the past several weeks, I've started to really consider that the elimination diet I did just made me sensitive to my already reactive foods. When I looked at this woman's website, I was also impressed by her diagrams showing what happens when a person eats something they don't tolerate. It resonates a lot with the information I've gathered from this website, as well as from my own personal experience.

You probably already know a reasonable list of foods that agree with you completely. You may want to try something similar to tease out the details.

Links: Dr. Oz show (can't believe I'm linking to his show from this website!!) http://www.doctoroz.com/videos/anti-diet-plan-eliminating-reactive-foods-pt-1
Diagrams on her website (simple, but sometimes I need simplicity!) http://lyngenet.com/wp-content/uploads/2012/11/How-Foods-Cause-Weight-Gain.pdf

### MamaGrok — 2013-01-31T05:53:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-73427>

Got two more results in. Not good.

Reverse T3 went from 314 to 282 to 455
(Free T3 went from 2.2 to 2.3 to 6.1)
(all labs 6 months apart)

I started natural desiccated thyroid between the first two lab sets, then increased it between the next two. Clearly I'm turning an awful lot of the T3 I'm taking into rT3 still, but it's definitely working, b/c most of the time, I feel a 7/10 instead of a 3/10 in mood & energy.

Last:
hsCRP went from .77 Jan '12 to 2.87 Jan'13.

I can't understand this at all. In Jan '12, I was coming off of a year with many accidental binges, and had been eating wheat once/week and dairy about the same for the last year or so. Even more in the last month b/c of Christmas.

Jan'13, I was coming off of a year with no intentional wheat AT ALL. No dairy until the last month (Christmas), and only rare even then. Almost no binges in the entire year. Was working on stress, cold, dark, & EMF exposure in mild, but intentional, ways, the entire year.

I don't understand this result at all.

I welcome all insights.

Hmmmm Mama, this is so strange. When my hsCRP was super high this time last year, jack indicated that it could be a biotoxin as cold tends to draw them out. I have no idea if this could be the case for you.

The thyroid thing is strange. If you are feeling good, it seems odd to reduce your intake - although you are on a super high dose. Perhaps your body doesnt need all the T3 so it isnt using it. The T3 level seems high. I would try to SLOWLY reduce you thyroid med and see if you can retain some of the energy. Keep in mind when reducing doses that you need to reduce a little bit at a time... let your body adjust (it might not feel good at first - expect to feel tired bc your body is probably down regulating thyroid hormone as you are already supplying - it needs to turn back on and replace it. I usually reduce..wait about 2 weeks, evaluate and tweak again.

I will have to keep thinking. Strange! So frustrating to be doing everything "Right" and not have the numbers reflect it. Have you been sick at all - any type of infection will affect the hsCRP.

### Lyndra — 2013-01-31T20:14:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-73500>

What's your RT3 to FT3 ratio? You want that to be at least 20. You didn't include units so I can't calculate that but you can do it at [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/](http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/).

Just guessing, your RT3 went up by 44% but FT3 went up by 165% so your ratio should be much improved. I stopped Armour because my ratio hit 22 and I was getting hyper, though my FT3 is still at the bottom of the range. Time will tell how that pans out for me.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-01-31T20:38:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-73507>

Yes you need to calculate the ratio. This will be a more accurate tale of what is going on. You may be fine on 4 grains of Naturthroid but I am highly suspect of the fillers as someone else mentioned for the possible increase in hsCRP.

Here is a list of the ingredients:

Colloidal Silicon Dioxide (from mined ore: natural desiccant to protect from moisture and humidity)
Dicalcium Phosphate (from mined ore, holds tablet together)
Lactose Monohydrate (traceable amount as part of desiccated thyroid powder USP) DIARY ALLERGY??[http://www.livestrong.com/article/370376-lactose-monohydrate-dairy-allergy/](http://www.livestrong.com/article/370376-lactose-monohydrate-dairy-allergy/)
Magnesium Stearate (from a vegetable source like palm oil; lubricating agent for tablet compress)
Microcrystalline Cellulose (synthetic fiber base to provide volume & bulk: also binds thyroid hormones, sadly)
Croscarmellose Sodium (aids in disintegration in stomach and sadly, even more cellulose!)
Stearic Acid (from vegetable source–typically palm oil; holds ingredients together)
Opadry II 85F19316 Clear
Porcine Thyroid Powder, U.S. Pharmacopeia (USP)

Check into that lactose ingredient, especially since you have been off diary for a little while. This maybe causing a reaction in your body thereby increasing the hsCRP. Another thing to consider is that some of the cellulose fillers maybe blocking your uptake of the FT3 in the NT and not allowing it all to go in, thus you have to take more.

### Aussie Nana — 2013-02-06T08:42:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-74398>

I suspect that your rT3 has climbed because you have more free T3 available and its shunting the extra into rT3. I'll be interested to watch this and see how you sort it out.

A thought came to mind. Some time back (1998) I felt great as I kept adding more and more NT. Then I got worried when I seemed to keep needing more and more to keep the good feelings and I was going downhill. So instead of adding more I started to slowly decrease it. It seemed that as long as I was varying my intake - either increasing or decreasing it - my body felt great. If I stayed at one level of NT after a few weeks I started to feel increasingly symptomatic. So for a while, until I ran out of my supply of NT I cycled up and down. Not sure that this info is helpful to anyone at all, but it was an indicator of how complicated this all is.

_FreeT3/rT3 ratio >20 7.8.13.33

Dzuganites, can you help me? Anyone else? What the FRAK is going on with my testosterone? Is that even possible?? What a horrible number! Yet here I am finally having a slight bit of libido after all these dry years...?

But finally nailed the thyroid dose, looks like. I'll keep it steady at 2.5 grains/day till my next check in 2-3 months.

### MamaGrok — 2013-03-15T01:29:18+0200

\- it was so high 2 months ago only because of the high NDT dose I was on for a short while

### Shijin13 — 2013-03-15T15:49:59+0200

MamaG

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" } [](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/hormone-cascade-jpg.55/)

you'll see that Testosterone converts to E2 (should be listed as Estradiol not esterone in the picture). so your Testosterone is being converted to Estrogen over on the BHRT thread [http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?1633-BHRT-Advice-suggestions-and/page58](http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?1633-BHRT-Advice-suggestions-and/page58) there's a great discussion going on about Estrogens - and how they can impact other hormones, thyroid included, especially if your Hypo. SHGB is going up - and in the BHRT Thread Joan mentions that as E goes up so will SHGB...

I also noticed your 17 Hydroyprogesterone has increased - so you might be having some pregnenolone steal occurring as well....

FWIW I cleared my RT3 using Armour thyroid - no T3 here... I'm deficient in T4, though armour coupled w/what little I'm making is at least converting to T3 now, instead of RT3.

IMO - I think you need to add pregnenolone, and increase your DHEA. you probably also need serious amounts of progesterone, you might need testosterone - if you can't get it up supplementing w/ pregnenolone and DHEA. I know you're not big on supplementation - but I'd consider going BIG with pregnenolone 100mg for 2-3mths and see where your at. I'd also pushing DHEA up to at least 50mg and adding 7Keto DHEA as well. Maca and Vitex/Chaste berry can help boost Progesterone in some women, usually not enough to off set E-dominance, though. I'm wondering if you might need the Pg in BHRT Form to help you with your cravings.... no-ones come up with that as a possible solution.... might be something worth thinking about...

### MamaGrok — 2013-03-15T16:25:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/got-more-labs-in-need-interpreters.5405/post-80667>

Thank you, Gretchen - I have been trying to find and use a proper Pg:E2 calculator for a long time, and I appreciate you doing those numbers for me! What is the good range for the ratio?

My E is still objectively low, though, isn't it? I think my problem is (if I understand right) less that E is increasing and more that Pg is not? certainly the ratio matters, but when both are dastardly low, I think that perhaps a different approach is warranted? I really don't know. I do know I need to find a Dzugan-like doctor stat. I was really hoping the DHEA supp would start to move the hormone numbers by itself, and it hasn't done squat.

One question I have is, why would all of a sudden so much T be shifted over to E2? This wasn't happening nearly to that extent before. What went wrong?

I only had the 17-OH Pg measured once, FWIW. I know the format I used is confusing.  It sure does look like I'm still making a lot of cortisol, even though my ASI showed nothing high, and it was decidedly low in the AM and a complete bottoming out at noon.

Now, it's been a long time since I read about supp, but what would be the reason to add 7-keto DHEA, as well? My impression was that that was only for women whose DHEA was converting disproportionately to T, either shown by labs or male-signs like hair darkening/thickening, etc.

What form should I consider taking the Pregn in? I think it comes over the counter, but is it better to get an Rx cream or something?

Thank you so much! This is obviously a problem I can no longer hope will go away. . DHEA just hasn't touched it yet.

range for Pg:E2 ratio 100-500, you want to be in the top Quartile. as far as E being low not entirely sure about that... I'm still trying to figure out E - but then again being I have PCOS I've got some Serious E-dominance issues along w/T dominance...

as for your T converting- to E - I think Pregnenolone steal might be responsible - so what ever DHEA you're using is converting to T then to E, b/c your not supplementing with Pregnenolone along with it.

I've had success using the LEF brand Pregnenolone, and the nutraceuticals Jansz recommends - personally I like the LEF better, less fillers. I buy most of my supplements from Amazon...

As far as Progesterone - you're going to need Rx Strength. you can call DzLogic - and talk w/Linda McHale about the program and see if they have a Dr in your area... the other thing you can do is also talk w/local compounding pharmacies about which doc's prescribe BHRT. the OTC won't really increase your Pg to the levels you're gonna need.

I'm taking both sublingual Pg, and topical - I'm on a pretty high dose - Dzugan thinks its too high - but my migraines and disrupted sleep during the last 5 days and first 5 days of my cycle disagree.

# Thread: metformin

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KiwiLauren — 2013-01-09T18:15:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin.5406/post-70093>

Anyone have any information, theories or thoughts on why metformin would cause loose stools/diarrea? This has been my experience after about a month+ taking it twice daily. I have since read many accounts of this happening to diabetics taking it (and who have found it so intolerable that they refuse to take it any longer as it was meaning they could never be far from a toilet). I am not diabetic. I was taking it for cancer-prevention, inflammation-reduction, etc (all the mechanisms Dr Kruse has talked about). I am trying to evaluate if I should continue taking this (I have currently stopped). My FBG is good, I have done huge work on gut healing with EPCOT Rx principles, my hsCRP is low, my HDL is good, my Pg:E ratio pretty good. My DHEA isn't great and I am hypothyroid. I am also taking d-Ribose.

I am wondering if the side effect means the medication is no longer needed? Or if there is some other mechanism(s) at work. What are your thoughts??? For those of you on metformin, any similar experiences? Thanks!

### Thor — 2013-01-09T18:29:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin.5406/post-70096>

.
Better check to see if you're positive for the genetic hickup called MTHFR - if you're positive then metformin can be deadly. Metformin sucks up all your B-12 which causes a number of problems. I use berberine as a better alternative that's safe.

Metformin-related vitamin B12 deficiency

http://ageing.oxfordjournals.org/content/35/2/200.full

also check out Dr Ben's Website - www mthfr net

>
> .
> Better check to see if you're positive for the genetic hickup called MTHFR - if you're positive then metformin can be deadly. Metformin sucks up all your B-12 which causes a number of problems. I use berberine as a better alternative that's safe.
> Metformin-related vitamin B12 deficiency
> http://ageing.oxfordjournals.org/content/35/2/200.full
> also check out Dr Ben's Website - www mthfr net

Thanks, Thor. I don't know if I have MTHFR nor when I would be able to get tested. But the B12 deficiency fits the symptoms. From the article you linked to it seems that one could become B12 deficient on metformin even without a methylation issue. Sounds like berberine doesn't cause the same problems. I'd rather take a non-pharmaceutical product anyway.

One thing I wonder about this theory... if the symptoms stop completely the day I stop taking metformin, can B12 respond so immediately?

### Thor — 2013-01-10T00:27:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin.5406/post-70122>

.
That's where it gets tricky - if you're not positive for MTHFR then you'll be fine on Methyl-B12 However there are a zillion genetic hickups that could be a problem. Which means you'll need a doc to help dial in which B-12 (Hyrdox or Methyl) and how much. If you're just low on
B-12 then it should hit fairly fast. I was getting admitted to hospice care for end of life treatment - within two doses of Dr Kendel Stewart, MD product for MTHFR I was almost normal - which blew my socks off. www Neurobiologix.com

Here's a partial list of other meds you should avoid if you're positive for MTHFR

Antacids, Protein Pump Inhibitors and Histamine Blockers
Many antacids contain aluminum. With the MTHFR gene mutation we have trouble clearing metals so aluminum is not a good thing to put in our bodies.
We also have trouble maintaining adequate levels of B12 (methylcobalamin not to be mistaken with cyanocobalamin). Aluminum can inhibit the absorption of B12.
Protein Pump Inhibitors decrease B12 and Folate (not to be mistaken with folic).
Histamine Blockers reduce the absorption of Folate and B12.

Colchine
Reduces the absorption of B12.

Oral diabetic medications
Metaformin
Causes malabsorption of B12, Folate and possibly B1 (thiamine)

Ethionamide
Inhibits B6 (P5P)

Cycloserine
Inhibits the absorption of Folate and inactivates B6.

Aminosalicylic acid
Reduces the absorption of Folate and B12 (methylcobalamin)

Antibiotics
Cephalosporins, Fluorquinolones, Isoniazid, Macrolides, Penicillan, Sulfonamides and Tetracyclines
Destroy intestinal gut flora
B1, B2, B5, B6, Folate and B12 all have decreased absorption.
People with MTHFR sometimes have problems clearing sulfates. Molybdenum seems to help people with MTHFR clear sulfates and sulfites as well.

Synthetic B vitamins
Many people I speak with who have MTHFR have problems with synthetic b vitamins. It actually jams up the active b vitamins from doing their job. I avoid vitamins with synthetic b's and foods fortified with synthetic b vitamins.
Synthetic B6 contains formaldahyde
Synthetic Folate known as Folic contains coal tar residue
Synthetic B12 known as cyanocobalamin contains cyanide phosphate 3'
These are not the active forms and with MTHFR you clearly need the active forms of b vitamins to manipulate this enzyme deficiency

Phenytoin and Fosphenytoin
Decreases the absorption of Folate and B12

Cholestyramine
Decreases the absorption of B12 and Folate.

Nitrous Oxide
Will cause B12 (methylcobalmin) deficiency in people with an MTHFR gene mutation that can possibly be severe. Prior to surgery and/or a dental visit it is important to let your doctor and anesthesiologist know that you cannot have nitrous oxide. Nitrous oxide can poduce hyperhomocystenemia and high homocysteine which in turn can cause a life threatning clotting event.

Pancreatic enzymes
Here is the deal with pancreatic enzymes. You can have low production of pancretic enzymes due to lack of nutrition and what pancreatic enzymes you have in your system can go into override and be depleted due to lack of nutrition. Pancreatic enzymes are needed for the methylation process. Medications and poor diet contribute to this. MTHFR and b deficiency is a clear marker of hypomethylation.

Phenobarbitol
Interferes with absorption of Folate

Cobalt
Can interfere with the absorption of B12

Chlorpromazine
Interferes with B2-riboflavin

Methylprednisone
Decreases Folate

Statin Drugs>Reduce glutathione levels. Many people with MTHFR already have compromised glutathione levels. Low glutathione levels can cause high homocysteine levels which in turn can lead to a clotting event.

Antidepressants/SSRI's
Signs of a Folate and B12 deficiency are sometimes anxiety and depression. If you know that you have an MTHFR gene mutation and suffer with anxiety and depression, shouldn't you try B12 (methylcobalimin) and Folate? Also BH4 is doing wonders for people who have the MTHFR gene mutation, anxiety and depression. TMG also helps with this. No one should ever stop taking their medication without first consulting a physician.

Cyclosporine
Cyclosporine and the MTHFR gene mutation has been found to elevate cardiovascular risk factors in the C677T gene mutation.

Methotrexate
Methotrexate is used to treat psoriasis and rheumatoid arthritis. It is known to cause Folate deficiency.

Vaccinations
People with the MTHFR deficiency sometimes have problems clearing metals. There are many toxic preservatives in vaccinations. Thimerasol and aluminum are found in many vaccinations. They can cause serious problems to someone with an MTHFR gene mutation. Thimerasol (mercury) and aluminum are known to deplete b vitamins. A person with MTHFR has trouble clearing metals. Mercury and aluminum absorb what b vitamins are in the body.

Amalgam Fillings
Amalgam fillings are made up of the toxic metal mercury. Someone with the MTHFR gene mutation who clearly cannot dump metals can have many problems with amalgam fillings. Anytime you chew food or put hot liquid into your mouth, a toxic mercury gas is let off in your mouth. Guess what is right above your mouth? YOUR BRAIN. Mercury depletes your B vitamins.

Fluoride
Fluoride has been linked to lowering glutathione.

SAM-e
Some people with MTHFR are not able to tolerate SAM-e
Side effects are dizziness, gas and headaches. SAM-e is unstable and can be inactive by the time you take it. TMG and BH4 work well in it's place.

Acetaminophen
Acetaminophen and glutathione deficiency
Many people with MTHFR have lower than normal glutathione levels.
B12 (methylcobalamin) and NAC (N-acetylcysteine) help to elevate glutathione. NAC does have some side effects. People with leaky gut syndrome, asthma and pulmonary hypertension should not use NAC.
In my opinion acetaminophen should be avoided by people who have low glutathione and the MTHFR gene mutation. There are studies out that show acetaminophen lowers your glutathione by 84%.

Diuretics
Diuretics have been linked to causing Thiamine (B1) deficiency.

Antifungals
Antifungals such as grisefulvin and fluconazole are hard on the liver. Glutathione is needed to clear out toxins and to maintain a healthy liver. With MTHFR and low glutathione it is best to avoid these drugs. Oil of oregano, coconut milk and a low carb diet are healthier alternatives that help keep yeast and candida away.

Fish Oil
Mercury tends to suck up our methylcobalamin (B12).
It is best to try other alternatives such as flaxseed oil and nattokinase (should not be taken with coumadin). Remember that many people with an MTHFR gene defect have problems clearing heavy metals and there is only one other heavy metal deadlier than mercury. It is plutonium.

Asthma medications
People with MTHFR sometimes need asthma medications. B12 (methylcobalamin) helps improve asthma symptoms. B12 is known as an antisulfite agent. Sulfites can cause asthma in people with MTHFR gene mutations because they have trouble converting sulfites. B6 promotes ATP and helps relax bronchial tissue.
Theophylline, albuterol and prednisone have been found to make us B6 deficient.

Anticonvulsants
Can cause B12 and B6 deficiency which can lead to anemia an fatigue. It can also elevate homocysteine and lead to stroke.

Neuroleptics
Neuroleptics can cause B12 deficiency and neuroleptic malignant syndrome.

Levodopa
Levodopa is used to treat Parkinson's.
It also decreases levels of B12 (methylcobalamin) and Folate.
This can cause polyneuropathy.

Antihypertensives
Antihypertension drugs interact with NAC (N-acetylcyteine). NAC helps produce glutathione. So if you are taking an antihypertensive drug, it is best not to put NAC in your MTHFR protocol.
Hydralazine increases the excretion of B6.

Oral contraceptives
Oral contraceptives have been found to drop folate by 40%. A person with the MTHFR gene mutation already has trouble keeping adequate amounts of folate in their body. Low levels of folate can elevate homocysteine and lead to a clotting event.

### caroline — 2013-01-10T00:46:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-lime-green-polyester-pant-suit.5408/post-70123>

I had a therapy app't today and we were talking about getting my life on track, meeting new people etc. etc. I said I had met a lovely woman - but - she is always wearing a Lime green polyester pant suit and I kinda said - somewhat tongue in cheek ... that I can't be friends with someone who wears a lime green polyester pant suit.
The therapist said I was being judgemental and somewhat elitist,
.To me - it is a metaphor

Thoughts?

### KiwiLauren — 2013-01-10T02:26:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-lime-green-polyester-pant-suit.5408/post-70125>

It sounds possibly like both you and your therapist are missing the same thing: curiosity. You are not curious about who this woman is (and have a predetermined notion/metaphor about what her clothing means); she is not curious about your metaphor and your reaction (and has a predetermined notion about what your thinking means). You are entitled to any metaphor or conclusion about a person you wish. It just might be interesting, however, to test that notion against a yet-undiscovered reality.

### endless — 2013-01-10T12:03:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-lime-green-polyester-pant-suit.5408/post-70163>

I have found that since participating in internet forums, I have become friends with people that had I met them in 'real life', I would never have even considered communicating with. Rebublicans, even...lol. I think that removing the visual cues lets you go deeper, and to search more authentically for common ground.

BTW, as far as the lime green polyester....I have those kind of judgements as well, about people who drive Hummers, or wear bedazzled kitty sweatshirts, or etc. etc.....BUT I have often been pleasantly suprised when I remember to set that pre-judgement aside.

### [email redacted] — 2013-01-10T21:02:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/love-my-new-doc.5420/post-70241>

So I finally bit the bullet and made an appointment with a new doc. I had high hopes as her web site mentioned BHRT, hormonal testing, being optimal. I went prepared with my list of labs,etc. and I was blown away with her knowledge. She knows about paleo way of eating and supports it. She recommended a full hormone work up, testing adrenals, thyroid and even recommended testing T3 and RT3. This is when I put my list of labs I wanted away cause she covered them all! She is recommending 2 weeks break from some of my current supplements prior to labs to get a good baseline test. And to top it off, she said I had the best looking tongue that she had seen in years ( to which she connected to the paleo diet). A couple things she said that I hadn't seen here- 1) to take fish oil at night as that will help alleviate hot flashes/night sweats until I can get lab results and 2) that it was important to break a sweat while exercising at least twice a week to keep metabolism up? Thoughts on those?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-12T08:41:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/love-my-new-doc.5420/post-70382>

lucky you and congrats. You might want to share her name for others.

# Thread: Why do circadian cycles matter to humans? Should we pay deep attn to them?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-do-circadian-cycles-matter-to-humans-should-we-pay-deep-attn-to-them.5429/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 29
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-11T09:50:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-do-circadian-cycles-matter-to-humans-should-we-pay-deep-attn-to-them.5429/post-70292>

For any organism to function properly, it must control the timing of its biological functions. That is why all higher organisms, from plants to insects, reptiles, birds and mammals, and even some lower unicellular forms of life such as bacteria, have developed an internal biological clock over millions of years. In humans, the master clock of all the bodyâ€™s biological clocks is located in the suprachiasmatic nucleus of the hypothalamus.

Therefore, they are designed to be rigid and tied to the light cycle and to the magnetic resonance of the earth. There is a big problem for modern humans. Those two axis have been radically altered in the last 100 years and our brain's control of both has been lost and the proof of it is ubiquotious. This is why 90% of Americans have hypothyroidism and many other hormone issues. This is a very important post. It is foundational for any one who comes to this site to learn. It is where all biology goes bad. It is not just a biochemical story.......it is a story of the loss of control of the cell cycle by the epigenetic signals that control our biology.

Yes, you must pay attn to them. For evolution to work, a cell first must adapt to its environment. So the first thing a cell would see in an earth day is a period of day and night. It also has to eat to make energy and it also has to control its own cellular division. So in essence the circadian cycle has to â€œyokeâ€￾ to the metabolic cycle and its growth cycle. When it is night time, the cell becomes more reduced chemically and electrically. The chemical reduction is measured in something call TAN. (total adenine nucleotides) The electrical reduction is measured by something called the zeta potential.

During a low redox time, cells are usually recycling their components using autophagy. During the day while energy is being made to explore the environment, the cell is more oxidized because of increased leakiness of the mitochondria. Another interesting coupling occurs between the circadian cycle with the cell cycle. They are linked via the PER 1 and PER 2 genes. PER 2 directly effects the cell cycle in mitosis.

Mitosis is the phase in the cell that occurs just before cell division to generate an offspring. The mammalian period 2 gene plays a key role in tumor growth in mice; mice with a mPER2 knockout show a significant increase in tumor development and a significant decrease in apoptosis (levee 19). This is thought to be caused by mPER2 circadian deregulation of common tumor suppression and cell cycle regulation genes, such as Cyclin D1, Cyclin A, Mdm-2, and Gadd45ÃŽÂ±, as well as the transcription factor c-myc, which is directly controlled by circadian regulators through E box-mediated reactions. This means that sleep is tied directly into to cell cycle functioning and directly into cell mediated immunity at some level. It appears that sleep directly effects the chronic diseases of aging and likely plays a role in all neolithic disease such as cancer development.

This information is foundational for anyone looking to reverse illness.

Many people here talk about IF and sleep. Few seem to understand they are both linked directly to the signals that control the circadian cycle. Those two signals are adenosine levels and alpha waves in the brain. These alpha waves come from magnetic cells in the SCN that are in complete resonance frequency with the earth's magnetic field. This is called Schumann resonance. The frequency of it is 7.83 hz and alpha waves at the SCN also resonante at the exact same frequency. This epigenetic signal is codified by the cells that control circadian cycles. Just as we pay attn to light and day we also pay attn to the earth's zeta potential.

Few realize that the final common pathway for sleep and IFing are the AMPk pathway (monitors TAN levels), adenosine, and alpha wave generation of the SCN. When ATP breaks down as we live our life, it ends up in AMP after hydrolysis. Then it breaks down further to inosine, hypoxanthine and xanthine, all of which also stimulate adenosine to replenish ATP. This is what should happen if our SCN is fine tuned to light and alpha waves. Melatonin levels tell us how yoked that system is in a human. In modern life rarely is it yoked for another ubiquitous reason I will be covering this year in my EMF Rx.

Adenosine opens the door to sleep but the alpha waves created in the SCN have to be present at the right time to allow it recycle ATP and our proteins. Timing is critical. This is why circadian biology trumps all the biochemistry. Alpha waves are the guardian of circadian cycling in the brain. When it is off all biochemistry is off in every cell because this cycle is wired to the growth and metabolic cycle of all cells.They are linked via the PER 1 and PER 2 genes. PER 2 directly effects the cell cycle in mitosis. I hope this helps you understand how powerfully important this is. This is foundational biology for my Quilt document at my site.

Hey Darleen.
Well it turns out our cold tap is earthed.

We have metal pipes and my husband measured the resistance between the tap and our mains earth with an ohm meter....err don't ask me what that is exactly.
So, with a metal clip on the cold tap attached by wire to an earthing wrist band ($5 from an electronics store) ...you're earthed.
He made a couple for the bedroom too....they're bands which have long stretchy, spiral, plastic coasted wires attached to a plug which is modified to connect to "earth" only and not the mains. they're plugged into a socket and turned off, because you don't need the switch to be turned on to connect to earth.
I can only tolerate earthing in the bath right now. Mitochondria, and detox support minerals need more work before I can fully earth.
It works though. I promise.

Have tried not to give too much thought to what anyone might think when they walk into our bedroom and see these wired up wristbands hanging off the headboard!
Anne.

### freesia — 2013-01-13T23:40:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-do-circadian-cycles-matter-to-humans-should-we-pay-deep-attn-to-them.5429/post-70619>

It's a revelation to me that apartments aren't earthed!
Here we have electric plugs with 3 prongs. The bottom prong is a connection to "earth". Each power socket is earthed.
If you modify a plug to disable the wires to the top two prongs you have a plug that connects to earth only. We have modified plugs (I think you can buy them pre-modified but my husband is handy like that so he made ours)...attached to stretchy wires then to a wrist band of the type people wear when they're working on sensitive electronic equipment. A few dollars from an electronics store. We have more power sockets than we use beside the bed so we keep the earthing bands plugged in...and the power switched off. Earth still works. Earth is GOOD like that...doesn't bump up your power bill! { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Other ways of earthing include connecting a wrist strap or earthing mat by wire to a copper stake pushed ino the ground outside. There is a website called lessemf dot com, I think they might illustrate a variety of options.

WAPF is interested in zeta potential maybe? A couple of people associated with them have mentioned zeta lately. But from what perspective? The way we use fat might be my best guess??

### Glen PDQ — 2013-01-14T13:04:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-do-circadian-cycles-matter-to-humans-should-we-pay-deep-attn-to-them.5429/post-70675>

Electrical receptacles can malfunction or be miswired. There is an inexpensive 120-volt tester that plugs into a receptacle. The 3 lights on the tester tell if it is correctly wired and grounded. It is very simple to use, just plug it in and look at the lights.

It is also possible an old receptacle has been updated from 2-prong to 3-prong without connecting a ground wire. Even though it has 3 prong holes, such an outlet will not supply ground.
